background_cases: A group of citizens peacefully refuses to pay a tax they regard as unjust, accepting the legal penalties.
Protesters occupy a public building to draw attention to racial segregation, submitting to arrest.
A conscientious objector publicly refuses military service and presents himself for trial.
definition: An act is one of civil disobedience if and only if (i) it is a deliberate violation of law, (ii) it is motivated by moral or political conviction, and (iii) the agent accepts the legal consequences of their action.
conditions: deliberate violation of law
motivated by moral conviction
accepts legal consequences
counterexample: A factory owner deliberately violates environmental regulations out of sincere moral conviction that the regulations are unjust, and willingly pays the resulting fines. The three conditions are met, yet we should not call this civil disobedience — the violation is private and self-interested, not a public appeal to the conscience of the community.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all public, communicative acts aimed at persuading fellow citizens. The definition dropped the publicity and communicative dimensions, admitting private acts of law-breaking that lack the political character of civil disobedience.
passage:
Consider the following cases of civil disobedience. A group of citizens peacefully refuses to pay a tax they regard as unjust, accepting the legal penalties. Protesters occupy a public building to draw attention to racial segregation, submitting willingly to arrest. A conscientious objector publicly refuses military service and presents himself for trial. From these cases one might extract a definition: an act is one of civil disobedience if and only if it is a deliberate violation of law, it is motivated by moral or political conviction, and the agent accepts the legal consequences of his action.

Yet this definition admits cases that do not seem to be civil disobedience at all. Suppose a factory owner sincerely believes that environmental regulations are unjust and deliberately violates them, willingly paying the resulting fines. He acts from moral conviction, violates the law deliberately, and accepts the consequences. The three conditions are met. Yet this is not civil disobedience; it is a private act of non-compliance undertaken for personal advantage under the cover of moral language. The background cases were all public, communicative acts — they were addressed to the conscience of the community, seeking to persuade fellow citizens of an injustice. The definition failed to capture this essential publicity and communicative purpose, admitting merely private law-breaking alongside genuinely political acts of protest.

background_cases: France exercises supreme authority within its borders, controls its military, and is recognised by other states.
The United Kingdom makes its own laws, commands its armed forces, and maintains diplomatic relations worldwide.
Japan governs its territory independently, has a national defence force, and is a member of international organisations.
definition: A state is sovereign if and only if (i) it exercises supreme authority within its territory, (ii) it commands a monopoly on the legitimate use of force, and (iii) it is recognised by other states.
conditions: supreme authority within territory
monopoly on legitimate force
recognised by other states
counterexample: A state recognised by the international community, which claims supreme authority and a monopoly on force, has in fact lost control of large portions of its territory to armed groups. The three conditions are nominally met, yet the state is not truly sovereign over its entire territory.
abductive_insight: The background cases involved states with effective control throughout their territory. The definition did not distinguish between claiming authority and effectively exercising it, admitting states whose sovereignty is nominal rather than actual.
passage:
Consider the following cases of sovereignty. France exercises supreme authority within its borders, controls its military, and is recognised by other states as sovereign. The United Kingdom makes its own laws, commands its armed forces, and maintains diplomatic relations worldwide. Japan governs its territory independently, has a national defence force, and is a member of international organisations. From these cases one might extract a definition: a state is sovereign if and only if it exercises supreme authority within its territory, commands a monopoly on the legitimate use of force, and is recognised by other states.

But consider a state which is recognised by the international community, which claims supreme authority and a nominal monopoly on force, yet which has in practice lost control of large portions of its territory to armed insurgent groups. The government issues decrees that are not enforced, collects no taxes outside the capital, and exercises no authority over the rebel-held regions. The three conditions appear nominally to be met — the state claims authority, claims a monopoly on force, and enjoys international recognition — yet it is not sovereign in any meaningful sense over much of its territory. The background cases all involved states with effective control throughout their territory; the definition failed to distinguish between the claim to authority and its effective exercise.

background_cases: The Edict of Nantes granted French Protestants the right to worship freely, and the resulting coexistence was widely regarded as a form of toleration.
In the Dutch Republic, Catholics, Jews, and various Protestant sects coexisted, each permitted to practise its faith, and the arrangement was celebrated as a model of toleration.
After the Toleration Act of 1689, English Dissenters were permitted to worship outside the established church, and this too was called toleration.
definition: A political arrangement constitutes toleration if and only if (i) the state permits practices it has the power to suppress, (ii) the permitted practices are ones the state or the dominant group regards as wrong or undesirable, and (iii) the permission is stable and not subject to arbitrary revocation.
conditions: state permits practices it could suppress
practices regarded as wrong or undesirable by state or dominant group
permission stable and not arbitrarily revocable
counterexample: A colonial power permits the religious practices of its subject peoples, regarding those practices as inferior. It has the power to suppress them and does not exercise it. The permission is stable and sustained over decades. The three conditions are met, yet the arrangement is better described as imperial condescension than as toleration, for it rests not on any recognition of the value of the tolerated practices but on the coloniser's calculation that suppression would be more trouble than it is worth.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved a degree of principled recognition — however reluctant — that the tolerated groups had some claim to practise as they did. The definition captured the structural features of these arrangements but dropped the normative dimension: that genuine toleration involves a principled reason for forbearance, not merely a pragmatic one.
passage:
Consider the following cases of toleration. The Edict of Nantes granted French Protestants the right to worship freely, and the resulting coexistence was widely regarded as a form of toleration. In the Dutch Republic, Catholics, Jews, and various Protestant sects coexisted, each permitted to practise its faith, and the arrangement was celebrated as a model of toleration. After the Toleration Act of 1689, English Dissenters were permitted to worship outside the established church, and this too was called toleration. From these cases one might extract a definition: a political arrangement constitutes toleration if and only if the state permits practices it has the power to suppress, the permitted practices are ones the state or the dominant group regards as wrong or undesirable, and the permission is stable and not subject to arbitrary revocation.

Yet this analysis admits of a troublesome case. Consider a colonial power which permits the religious practices of its subject peoples. The coloniser regards these practices as primitive and inferior. It has the military and administrative apparatus to suppress them. The permission is stable, sustained over decades as a matter of settled policy. The three conditions are satisfied. Yet the arrangement seems less like toleration than like imperial condescension. The coloniser does not permit the practices because it recognises any claim the subject peoples have to practise them; it permits them because suppression would provoke rebellion and disrupt the extraction of resources. The background cases — the Edict of Nantes, the Dutch Republic, the Toleration Act — all involved, however imperfectly, a recognition that the tolerated groups had some principled claim to practise as they did. The definition captured the structural features of these arrangements but dropped this normative dimension, admitting cases of mere pragmatic forbearance alongside genuine toleration.

background_cases: A member of Parliament elected by the citizens of a constituency speaks on their behalf in the House; we say he represents them.
A trade union delegate chosen by the workers of a factory negotiates with management on their behalf; he too represents them.
An ambassador appointed by his government speaks for his nation in foreign courts; he represents the nation.
definition: A person represents a group if and only if (i) they have been authorised by the group to act on its behalf, (ii) their actions are binding upon the group, and (iii) the group has a mechanism for holding them to account.
conditions: authorised by group to act on its behalf
actions binding upon group
group has mechanism for holding them to account
counterexample: A self-appointed leader of a protest movement, without any formal authorisation, speaks to the press on behalf of the movement. His statements are not formally binding, and the movement has no mechanism for holding him to account. Yet he articulates the movement's grievances so faithfully that the members recognise his statements as their own. He represents the movement in a substantive sense, though none of the three conditions is satisfied.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved formal institutions of authorisation — elections, appointments, mandates. The definition captured the institutional trappings of representation but missed the possibility of substantive representation without formal authorisation, where a person represents a group by virtue of faithfully articulating its views and interests.
passage:
Consider three cases of representation. A member of Parliament elected by the citizens of a constituency speaks on their behalf in the House; we say he represents them. A trade union delegate chosen by the workers of a factory negotiates with management on their behalf; he too represents them. An ambassador appointed by his government speaks for his nation in foreign courts; he represents the nation. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person represents a group if and only if he has been authorised by the group to act on its behalf, his actions are binding upon the group, and the group has a mechanism for holding him to account.

But consider a case which tells against this analysis. A protest movement arises spontaneously in a large city. It has no formal structure, no elected leaders, and no constitution. Yet one man, by virtue of his eloquence and his intimate understanding of the movement's grievances, begins to speak to the press on its behalf. He has received no authorisation; his statements are not formally binding upon the movement; and there is no mechanism by which the participants might hold him to account. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet the members of the movement recognise his statements as faithful expressions of their views, and the public comes to understand the movement's aims through his words. He represents the movement in a substantive sense. The background cases were all instances of formal, institutional representation — elections, appointments, mandates. The definition captured what these cases had in common but missed the possibility that representation may arise informally, through the faithful articulation of a group's views, without any institutional apparatus whatever.

background_cases: A citizen who has voted in elections, used public roads, and been protected by the police is said to have an obligation to obey the laws of his state.
A person who has accepted a scholarship from a government and pledged to serve the country afterwards is said to have an obligation to honour his pledge.
Members of a cooperative who have all contributed labour and shared the harvest are said to owe one another compliance with the cooperative's rules.
definition: A person has a political obligation to obey a rule if and only if (i) they have voluntarily accepted benefits from the institution that enacted the rule, (ii) the institution is reasonably just, and (iii) the rule was enacted through fair procedures.
conditions: voluntarily accepted benefits from institution
institution is reasonably just
rule enacted through fair procedures
counterexample: A hermit who lives on remote public land has never voted, never used public roads, and has declined all state services. He grows his own food, builds his own shelter, and asks nothing of the government. He has not voluntarily accepted any benefits. A new law requires all residents to register their dwellings. The first condition fails, for he has accepted no benefits. Yet one might argue he still has a political obligation to obey the law, since he occupies territory over which the state exercises jurisdiction.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved persons who had clearly participated in and benefited from cooperative arrangements. The definition generalised from these cases a principle of reciprocity, but it failed to account for the obligation that may arise from mere residence within a jurisdiction, independently of any voluntary acceptance of benefits.
passage:
Consider three cases in which political obligation seems clear. A citizen who has voted in elections, used public roads, and been protected by the police is said to have an obligation to obey the laws of his state. A person who has accepted a government scholarship and pledged to serve afterwards is said to owe compliance with the terms of his pledge. Members of a cooperative who have contributed labour and shared the harvest are said to owe one another adherence to the cooperative's rules. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person has a political obligation to obey a rule if and only if he has voluntarily accepted benefits from the institution that enacted the rule, the institution is reasonably just, and the rule was enacted through fair procedures.

Yet this analysis encounters difficulty with the following case. A hermit lives on remote public land, far from any settlement. He has never voted, never used a public road, and has declined every service the state has offered. He grows his own food, draws his own water, and builds his own shelter. He has not voluntarily accepted any benefit from the state. A new law requires all residents to register their dwellings with the local authority. The first condition of our definition fails: the hermit has accepted no benefits. Yet there is some force to the argument that he is nevertheless obligated to comply, for he occupies territory over which the state exercises jurisdiction, and his very security — his freedom from invasion and assault — depends, whether he acknowledges it or not, upon the state's existence. The background cases were all instances of clear, voluntary participation in cooperative schemes. The definition abstracted a principle of reciprocity from these cases but could not accommodate the obligation that may attach to mere residence within a jurisdiction, independently of any voluntary act of acceptance.

background_cases: Workers who contribute more hours to a collective enterprise receive larger shares of the product; this is thought just.
Students who perform better on examinations receive higher marks and greater academic honours; this is thought just.
Athletes who train harder and perform better win the prizes; this too strikes us as just.
definition: A distribution is just if and only if (i) shares are allocated according to individual merit or contribution, (ii) the criteria for assessing merit are applied consistently, and (iii) the allocation is transparent and publicly known.
conditions: shares allocated according to merit or contribution
criteria applied consistently
allocation transparent and publicly known
counterexample: Two workers contribute equally to an enterprise and receive equal shares. The criteria are applied consistently and the allocation is transparent. Yet one worker began from a position of inherited wealth and extensive education, while the other overcame severe deprivation and disability to reach the same level of contribution. Equal treatment according to merit yields identical rewards, yet one feels that justice has not been done, for the circumstances of the two workers are so different that equal treatment produces an inequitable result.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved agents competing on roughly equal footing. The definition captured the merit principle operative in those cases but dropped the background condition of equal starting points, failing to account for the way prior disadvantage affects the moral significance of equal contribution.
passage:
Consider three cases of just distribution. Workers who contribute more hours to a collective enterprise receive larger shares of the product; this is thought just. Students who perform better on examinations receive higher marks; this too is thought just. Athletes who train harder and perform better win the prizes; again, this strikes us as fair. From these cases one might extract a definition: a distribution is just if and only if shares are allocated according to individual merit or contribution, the criteria for assessing merit are applied consistently, and the allocation is transparent and publicly known.

Yet consider two workers who contribute equally to a cooperative enterprise and receive, in accordance with the definition, equal shares. The criteria are applied with perfect consistency, and the allocation is fully transparent. But one of the workers began from a position of inherited wealth, excellent health, and extensive education, whilst the other overcame severe poverty, a physical disability, and the absence of formal schooling to reach the same level of output. The merit principle, applied consistently, yields identical rewards. Yet one senses that something is amiss: the second worker's equal contribution represents a far greater achievement than the first's, and treating them identically ignores this morally relevant difference. The background cases from which we drew our definition all involved agents competing on roughly equal footing — workers in the same factory, students in the same school, athletes in the same event. The definition abstracted the merit principle from these cases but dropped the implicit assumption of roughly equal starting conditions, and accordingly fails to account for cases where prior disadvantage renders formal equality of treatment substantively unjust.

background_cases: A government bans the publication of a novel deemed seditious; we call this censorship.
A royal censor excises passages from a theatrical script before it may be performed; this too is censorship.
A state prohibits the import of foreign newspapers critical of the regime; again, censorship.
definition: An act constitutes censorship if and only if (i) the content of an expression is suppressed or altered, (ii) the suppression is carried out by an authority external to the author, and (iii) the suppression is motivated by a judgement that the content is harmful or dangerous.
conditions: content suppressed or altered
suppression by authority external to author
motivated by judgement that content is harmful or dangerous
counterexample: A writer, knowing that the state will punish any expression of dissent, alters his own manuscript before submission, removing every passage that might give offence. No external authority has acted; the content was suppressed by the author himself. Yet the suppression was motivated by the state's known judgement that dissent is dangerous, and the effect on public discourse is identical to that of overt censorship. The second condition fails, for no external authority has suppressed anything, yet the case is plainly one of censorship in all but form.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved overt acts of suppression by an identifiable external authority. The definition captured this visible, institutional pattern but missed the phenomenon of self-censorship, in which the mere threat of suppression produces the same result without any overt act by the censor.
passage:
Consider three cases of censorship. A government bans the publication of a novel deemed seditious; we call this censorship. A royal censor excises passages from a theatrical script before it may be performed; this too is censorship. A state prohibits the import of foreign newspapers critical of the regime; again, censorship. From these cases one might define censorship: an act constitutes censorship if and only if the content of an expression is suppressed or altered, the suppression is carried out by an authority external to the author, and the suppression is motivated by a judgement that the content is harmful or dangerous.

But consider a writer living under an authoritarian regime. He knows that any expression of dissent will be punished — perhaps by imprisonment, perhaps by worse. Before submitting his manuscript, he carefully removes every passage that might give offence, substituting anodyne phrases for dangerous ones. No external authority has acted upon his manuscript; no censor has read it; no government official has suppressed a word. The suppression was carried out entirely by the author himself. The second condition of the definition fails. Yet the effect on public discourse is precisely the same as if the censor had excised the offending passages with his own hand. The background cases were all instances of overt, institutional censorship — banning, excision, prohibition. The definition captured this visible pattern but missed the subtler and perhaps more pervasive form of censorship that operates through anticipation and fear, in which the author becomes his own censor and the state need not lift a finger.

background_cases: In Athens, the citizen assembly voted directly on laws, and the resulting legislation reflected the preferences of the majority of those present; the system is widely regarded as democratic.
In the Swiss cantons, citizens gather periodically to vote on matters of public concern, and the majority's decision binds the community; this too is considered a form of democracy.
In a New England town meeting, all residents deliberate and vote on local ordinances, with the majority prevailing; the practice is held up as an example of direct democratic governance.
definition: A political system is democratic if and only if (i) the citizens themselves vote directly on laws, (ii) each citizen's vote carries equal weight, and (iii) the majority's decision is binding on all.
conditions: the citizens themselves vote directly on laws
each citizen's vote carries equal weight
the majority's decision is binding on all
counterexample: Consider a modern nation-state of fifty million inhabitants. The citizens do not vote directly on laws; instead, they elect representatives who deliberate and legislate on their behalf. No citizen votes on any particular statute. Yet the state is universally regarded as a democracy. It holds free elections, its representatives are accountable to the electorate, and the government may be changed by popular vote. The first condition fails — the citizens do not vote directly on laws — yet the system is democratic.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of direct democracy in small polities — an ancient city-state, small cantons, rural townships — where it is feasible for citizens to assemble and vote on laws in person. The definition abstracted the directness of citizen voting as essential to democracy, because in every case examined the citizens did indeed vote on laws themselves. But the background cases were drawn from a restricted class of small-scale polities. In larger states, direct voting on every law is impracticable, and democracy takes the form of representative government, in which citizens choose legislators rather than legislating themselves. The essential feature of democracy is not direct citizen voting but the derivation of governmental authority from the will of the governed, whether that will is expressed directly or through elected representatives.
passage:
Consider three instances of democratic governance. In Athens, the citizen assembly voted directly on matters of legislation, and the decisions of the majority were binding upon the whole city. In the Swiss cantons, citizens have long gathered to vote on questions of public concern, with each man's vote counting equally and the majority's verdict determining the outcome. In the New England town meeting, all residents assemble to deliberate and to vote upon local ordinances, and the majority prevails. From these cases one might extract a definition: a political system is democratic if and only if the citizens themselves vote directly on laws, each citizen's vote carries equal weight, and the majority's decision is binding upon all. In every background case, the citizens did indeed vote directly, each vote counted equally, and the majority ruled.

But consider a modern nation-state of fifty million inhabitants. Its citizens do not vote upon any particular statute; they elect representatives who meet in a legislative assembly and enact laws on the citizens' behalf. No citizen votes directly on the content of any law. The first condition of the definition fails. Yet the state is universally acknowledged to be a democracy — indeed, it is often held up as an exemplary one. Its elections are free and fairly conducted, its representatives are accountable to the electorate, and the government may be peacefully replaced by popular vote. The background cases were all drawn from small-scale polities — a city-state, rural cantons, country townships — in which it was practicable for citizens to gather in one place and vote upon laws in person. The definition mistook a feature of these small polities — the directness of citizen participation — for the essence of democracy itself. What makes a system democratic is not that citizens vote on every law but that governmental authority derives, by some regular and accountable mechanism, from the will of the governed. Representative government satisfies this requirement no less than direct assembly, and any definition that excludes it has confused a particular institutional form with the principle it embodies.

background_cases: A man imprisoned in a gaol is unfree: physical walls and locked doors prevent him from going where he wishes.
A serf bound to his lord's estate by law is unfree: a legal prohibition prevents him from leaving the land he works.
A slave held in chains is unfree: both physical restraint and the legal institution of slavery deprive him of liberty.
definition: A person is unfree if and only if (i) a physical barrier prevents them from acting as they wish, (ii) a law or command of another person prohibits their action, and (iii) the constraint is imposed by an identifiable human agent.
conditions: a physical barrier prevents them from acting as they wish
a law or command prohibits their action
the constraint is imposed by an identifiable human agent
counterexample: Consider a person who lives in a society with no legal prohibition on travel, no physical barrier obstructing his movement, and no identifiable person preventing him from leaving his district. Yet he is so poor that he cannot afford the fare for any conveyance, he has no food for a journey on foot, and every employer in neighbouring districts refuses to hire persons of his social class — not by law, but by entrenched custom. He is, for all practical purposes, confined to his district as surely as if walls surrounded it. No condition of the definition is met, yet he is profoundly unfree.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved unfreedom produced by identifiable agents through physical or legal coercion — a gaoler, a feudal lord, a slave-owner. The definition abstracted these features as the essence of unfreedom: physical barriers, legal prohibitions, and identifiable human agents. But the background cases were drawn from a class of particularly vivid and overt constraints. The man confined by poverty and custom is constrained by structural conditions — the distribution of wealth, entrenched social practices — that lack a single identifiable agent and operate without physical walls or legal commands. Unfreedom may arise not only from overt coercion by identifiable persons but from impersonal social and economic structures that restrict a person's options as effectively as any chain or statute.
passage:
Consider three cases of unfreedom. A man imprisoned in a gaol is unfree: physical walls and locked doors confine him, and a gaoler enforces his confinement. A serf bound by law to his lord's estate is unfree: a legal command prevents him from leaving the land he works, and the lord enforces this command through the institutions of feudal society. A slave held in chains is unfree by the most direct means: physical restraint and the legal institution of slavery deprive him of all liberty, and his master is the identifiable agent of his subjection. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person is unfree if and only if a physical barrier prevents him from acting as he wishes, a law or command of another person prohibits his action, and the constraint is imposed by an identifiable human agent.

But consider a person who inhabits a society in which no law prohibits his movement, no wall obstructs his path, and no identifiable person stands between him and his destination. He may go where he pleases — in principle. In practice, he cannot. He is destitute: he possesses no money for transport, no provisions for a journey, and no prospect of employment in any neighbouring district, for employers there decline to hire persons of his social class, not because the law forbids it, but because custom dictates it. He is confined to his district as effectively as if a wall surrounded it, yet no physical barrier exists, no law prohibits his departure, and no single human agent imposes the constraint. The definition, drawn from cases of overt coercion by identifiable persons, has overlooked the possibility that unfreedom may arise from the impersonal operation of economic deprivation and social custom. The prisoner, the serf, and the slave are unfree by virtue of chains, laws, and masters; the destitute man is unfree by virtue of a social structure that, without any identifiable agent or explicit prohibition, renders his formal liberty meaningless.

background_cases: In a feudal society, nobles are exempt from taxation whilst commoners bear the full burden; this inequality is codified in law and universally regarded as unjust.
In a caste system, members of lower castes are legally barred from certain occupations and forced to perform menial labour; the legal inequality is manifest and widely condemned.

Under a regime of racial segregation, one racial group is legally prohibited from attending the same schools, using the same facilities, and residing in the same districts as another; the inequality is written into statute.
definition: A society is characterised by unjust inequality if and only if (i) the law explicitly assigns different rights or duties to different groups, (ii) the distinction is based upon an ascribed characteristic such as birth, caste, or race, and (iii) the disadvantaged group is legally compelled to accept its inferior status.
conditions: the law explicitly assigns different rights or duties to different groups
the distinction is based upon an ascribed characteristic
the disadvantaged group is legally compelled to accept its inferior status
counterexample: Consider a society in which the law assigns identical rights to every citizen regardless of birth, caste, or race. No statute distinguishes among groups, and no one is legally compelled to accept an inferior status. Yet the educational system is funded by local property taxes, with the consequence that children in wealthy districts receive an education vastly superior to that available in poor districts. The residents of poor districts are disproportionately members of a single ethnic group, owing to historical patterns of settlement. The inequality is systematic, persistent, and closely correlated with ethnicity, yet it arises not from any explicit legal distinction but from the intersection of a facially neutral funding mechanism with a historically produced pattern of residential segregation. No condition of the definition is met, yet the inequality is, by any reasonable assessment, unjust.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of de jure inequality — inequality written into law and imposed upon groups defined by ascribed characteristics. The definition abstracted legal explicitness, ascribed characteristics, and legal compulsion as the marks of unjust inequality. But the background cases were drawn from a class of particularly overt injustices. A society may exhibit systematic inequality that is closely correlated with ascribed characteristics yet arises not from explicit legal distinctions but from the operation of facially neutral institutions upon historically produced social patterns. The definition mistakes the most visible form of unjust inequality for the only form, when in truth structural and institutional mechanisms can produce inequalities no less systematic and no less unjust than those imposed by explicit law.
passage:
Consider three societies marked by unjust inequality. In a feudal order, the law exempts nobles from taxation and imposes the full burden upon commoners; the distinction is based upon birth, and the commoners have no legal recourse. In a caste system, members of lower castes are barred by law from certain occupations and compelled to perform menial labour; the distinction is ascribed at birth and enforced by statute. Under a regime of racial segregation, one racial group is legally prohibited from sharing schools, facilities, and residential districts with another; the inequality is codified and compulsory. From these cases one might define unjust inequality as obtaining when the law explicitly assigns different rights or duties to different groups, the distinction is based upon an ascribed characteristic, and the disadvantaged group is legally compelled to accept its inferior status.

But consider a society in which the law assigns identical rights to every citizen. No statute mentions birth, caste, or race. No one is legally compelled to accept an inferior position. Yet the educational system is funded by local property taxes, and the distribution of property is starkly unequal: wealthy districts, populated largely by one ethnic group, spend five times as much per pupil as poor districts, populated largely by another. The residents of poor districts are disproportionately members of a single ethnicity, a pattern traceable to historical practices of exclusion that have since been formally abolished. The children of the poor districts receive an education so inferior that their prospects for economic advancement are severely curtailed, and the cycle perpetuates itself across generations. No explicit legal distinction is drawn; no ascribed characteristic is mentioned in any statute; no group is legally compelled to accept its position. Yet the inequality is systematic, persistent, and closely bound to ethnicity. The definition, derived from cases of overt legal discrimination, cannot recognise this form of injustice, for it identifies unjust inequality exclusively with explicit legal distinctions. What the definition has failed to apprehend is that facially neutral institutions, operating upon a landscape shaped by historical injustice, may produce inequalities no less systematic and no less morally objectionable than those inscribed in the statute book.

background_cases: A general commands his troops to advance, and they obey because he holds a commission from the sovereign and occupies a position within the military hierarchy; his authority derives from his office.
A judge sentences a convicted criminal, and the sentence is enforced because the judge occupies a position within the judicial system established by law; her authority is institutional.
A headmaster directs the conduct of pupils in his school, and they comply because he has been appointed to his position by the governing body; his authority rests upon institutional appointment.
definition: A person X has authority over person Y if and only if (i) X occupies an institutional office, (ii) Y falls within the jurisdiction of that office, and (iii) X's directives are issued in the exercise of that office's recognised functions.
conditions: X occupies an institutional office
Y falls within the jurisdiction of that office
X's directives are issued in the exercise of the office's functions
counterexample: Consider a community that has no formal institutions — no offices, no hierarchies, no constitutions. Yet one member of the community, an elderly woman of long experience and conspicuous wisdom, is regularly consulted on disputes, and her judgements are accepted and acted upon by the other members. She holds no office; the community has no offices to hold. No one falls within her jurisdiction, for she has none. Her authority rests entirely upon the respect she commands by virtue of her personal qualities. Yet she has authority in a perfectly intelligible sense: her pronouncements are treated as settling matters, and her counsel is followed. No condition of the definition is satisfied, yet authority obtains.
abductive_insight: The background cases — a general, a judge, a headmaster — were all instances of institutional authority, in which a person's right to command derived from an office within a formal hierarchy. The definition abstracted institutional office, jurisdiction, and functional scope as the essence of authority. But the background cases were drawn from highly institutionalised settings. Authority may also arise from personal qualities — wisdom, experience, moral stature — in the absence of any institutional framework. The elderly woman's authority is real and effective, yet it rests upon no office and operates within no jurisdiction. The definition has mistaken one species of authority — the institutional — for the genus, overlooking the possibility of authority grounded in personal qualities recognised by a community.
passage:
Consider three instances of authority. A general commands his troops to advance, and they obey: his authority derives from his commission, his rank, his position within a military hierarchy sanctioned by the sovereign. A judge sentences a convicted criminal, and the sentence is carried out: her authority rests upon her appointment to the bench, her jurisdiction over the matter before her, and the institutional framework of the courts. A headmaster directs the conduct of pupils, and they comply: his authority arises from his appointment by the governing body and the recognised functions of his office. From these cases one might conclude that authority obtains when a person occupies an institutional office, the person over whom authority is exercised falls within the jurisdiction of that office, and the directives issued are within the office's recognised functions.

But consider a community that possesses no formal institutions whatsoever — no constitutions, no offices, no hierarchies, no written rules. Within this community lives an elderly woman of exceptional wisdom and long experience. When disputes arise, the members of the community come to her for counsel. She listens, deliberates, and pronounces her judgement. Her judgements are accepted — not because any institution compels acceptance, but because the community recognises in her a quality of discernment that commands respect. She holds no office; the community has none to offer. No one falls within her jurisdiction, for she possesses no jurisdiction. Her directives are not issued in the exercise of any recognised function, for no function has been formally defined. Yet her authority is real: her pronouncements settle disputes, her counsel guides conduct, and her judgement is preferred to that of any other member. The definition, drawn from cases of institutional authority in formally organised settings, cannot accommodate this phenomenon. Authority, it would appear, may rest not only upon institutional office but upon personal qualities — wisdom, experience, moral seriousness — that a community recognises and defers to, and a definition that confines authority to institutions has captured only one of its forms.

background_cases: The government of a stable constitutional monarchy has ruled for centuries under a succession of kings who ascended to the throne according to established rules of hereditary succession; its legitimacy is widely accepted.
A republic established by revolution has governed for several generations under a constitution ratified by a constituent assembly; its legitimacy is generally acknowledged.
A parliamentary democracy has held free elections at regular intervals for decades, with orderly transitions of power; its legitimacy is unquestioned.
definition: A government is legitimate if and only if (i) it has governed continuously for a substantial period, (ii) its mode of succession or selection is well established and predictable, and (iii) the population does not actively resist its rule.
conditions: it has governed continuously for a substantial period
its mode of succession or selection is well established and predictable
the population does not actively resist its rule
counterexample: Consider a newly formed government that has existed for only a matter of weeks. It was established by a popular uprising against a tyrannical regime, and a provisional council, broadly representative of the population, has assumed power. Its mode of succession is not yet established, for it is a new creation. And a portion of the population — the former regime's loyalists — actively resists its rule. None of the three conditions is satisfied. Yet many would judge this provisional government legitimate, for it arose from a justified revolution against an oppressive regime and governs with the broad consent of the people it liberated.
abductive_insight: The background cases — a constitutional monarchy, an established republic, a long-standing parliamentary democracy — were all instances of governments that had persisted over long periods with stable succession mechanisms and acquiescent populations. The definition abstracted longevity, predictability, and absence of resistance as the marks of legitimacy. But the background cases were drawn from a class of mature, stable regimes. A government born of justified revolution may be legitimate from its inception, even though it is new, its institutions are unformed, and some portion of the population resists. Legitimacy is not merely a function of persistence and stability but may derive from the justice of a government's origin and the consent of the governed, conditions that can obtain even in the absence of longevity and settled procedures.
passage:
Consider three governments whose legitimacy is widely acknowledged. A constitutional monarchy has ruled for centuries, its kings ascending to the throne according to established rules of hereditary succession; the continuity of the institution lends it an air of settled authority. A republic, established by revolution several generations past, has governed under a constitution ratified by a broadly representative assembly; its procedures are well known and its transitions of power orderly. A parliamentary democracy has held free elections at regular intervals for decades, and no government has assumed power except through the ballot box. From these cases one might extract a definition: a government is legitimate if and only if it has governed continuously for a substantial period, its mode of succession or selection is well established and predictable, and the population does not actively resist its rule.

But consider a government that has existed for a mere three weeks. It arose from a popular uprising against a regime that had for years imprisoned political opponents, censored the press, and confiscated the property of disfavoured groups. A provisional council, drawn from diverse elements of the population and broadly representative of the society, has assumed the functions of government. Its mode of succession is not yet established, for it is an improvisation born of necessity. And a portion of the population — the functionaries and beneficiaries of the former regime — actively resists its rule. Not one of the three conditions is satisfied: the government is new, its procedures are unformed, and resistance persists. Yet one might reasonably judge it legitimate, for it arose from a justified rebellion against manifest tyranny and governs with the broad consent of those it has liberated. The definition, having drawn its conditions from the features of mature and stable regimes, has confused the marks of successful governance with the grounds of legitimacy itself. Legitimacy may be present at the very moment of a government's founding, if that founding is rooted in justice and consent, and no definition that requires longevity and settled procedure can account for this possibility.

background_cases: France under Louis XIV exercised supreme authority within its borders, maintained a powerful army, and recognised no superior in either domestic or foreign affairs; it was sovereign in the fullest sense.
The Ottoman Empire at its zenith governed a vast territory, commanded formidable military forces, and negotiated with other powers as an equal; its sovereignty was unquestioned.
The Kingdom of Prussia in the eighteenth century enacted its own laws, maintained its own army, and conducted an independent foreign policy; it was a sovereign state.
definition: A state is sovereign if and only if (i) it possesses a centralised government with supreme authority over its territory, (ii) it commands military forces adequate to defend its borders, and (iii) it conducts foreign policy independently of any other state.
conditions: it possesses a centralised government with supreme authority
it commands adequate military forces
it conducts foreign policy independently
counterexample: Consider the member states of a federal union — say, a federation in which each constituent state retains its own legislature, its own courts, and its own body of law, but in which matters of defence and foreign policy are reserved to the federal government. No member state commands its own military forces or conducts its own foreign policy. The central government exercises supreme authority in certain domains, but the member states exercise supreme authority in others. The second and third conditions fail for the member states, and arguably the first condition is satisfied by neither the member states individually nor the federal government alone, since supreme authority is divided. Yet the federation as a whole is sovereign, and the member states exercise a form of autonomy that is not easily captured by the definition.
abductive_insight: The background cases — absolutist France, the Ottoman Empire, eighteenth-century Prussia — were all instances of unitary states with highly centralised governments, powerful armies, and independent foreign policies. The definition abstracted centralisation, military adequacy, and foreign policy independence as the essence of sovereignty. But the background cases were drawn from unitary, absolutist or near-absolutist states. Federal systems divide authority between a central government and constituent units in ways that the definition cannot accommodate: neither the federal government alone nor the member states individually satisfy all three conditions, yet sovereignty resides in the federal arrangement as a whole. Sovereignty, it appears, may be divided, shared, or layered in ways that a definition modelled on unitary states cannot capture.
passage:
Consider three states whose sovereignty was, in their respective eras, beyond dispute. France under Louis XIV exercised supreme and centralised authority within its borders, maintained the most powerful army in Europe, and recognised no superior in domestic or foreign affairs. The Ottoman Empire at its zenith governed a vast territory from a single capital, commanded formidable military forces, and negotiated with other powers as an equal. The Kingdom of Prussia in the eighteenth century enacted its own laws, sustained its own army, and conducted its foreign policy with complete independence. From these cases one might define sovereignty as follows: a state is sovereign if and only if it possesses a centralised government with supreme authority over its territory, it commands military forces adequate to defend its borders, and it conducts foreign policy independently of any other state.

But consider a federal union comprising several dozen constituent states. Each member state has its own legislature, its own courts, and its own body of law governing matters such as education, property, and criminal justice. Yet no member state maintains its own army or conducts its own foreign policy; these functions are reserved to the federal government. The federal government, in turn, lacks authority over the domains reserved to the states. Supreme authority is not centralised in any single institution but divided between two levels of government, each supreme within its own sphere. The second and third conditions of the definition fail for the member states, and the first condition is, at best, only partially satisfied by the federal government, which is supreme in some matters and subordinate in others. Yet the federation as a whole is sovereign — it defends its borders, conducts foreign relations, and answers to no external authority. The definition, drawn from cases of unitary and centralised states, presupposes that sovereignty must be lodged in a single centralised authority. It cannot accommodate the reality of federal sovereignty, in which supreme authority is divided, shared, and exercised at multiple levels — a form of political organisation that the absolutist background cases simply did not exhibit.

background_cases: A group of citizens refuses to pay a tax they regard as unjust, publicly announcing their refusal and accepting the penalties imposed by the courts; their protest is directed against a specific fiscal policy.
Workers in a factory refuse to comply with a labour regulation they consider oppressive, publicly declaring their reasons and submitting to arrest; their protest targets a particular statute.

Residents of a district refuse to comply with a zoning ordinance that they believe unjustly restricts their property rights, openly defying the regulation and accepting the fines; their protest addresses a specific local law.
definition: An act is one of civil disobedience if and only if (i) it is a deliberate and public refusal to comply with a specific law or regulation, (ii) the agent accepts the legal penalties for noncompliance, and (iii) the aim is to bring about the repeal or amendment of that specific law or regulation.
conditions: it is a deliberate and public refusal to comply with a specific law
the agent accepts the legal penalties
the aim is to bring about the repeal or amendment of that specific law
counterexample: Consider a citizen who publicly and deliberately refuses to comply with a conscription law, accepts the legal consequences of his refusal, but whose aim is not to bring about the repeal of the conscription statute. His aim is rather to bear witness to a moral principle — that the taking of human life in war is always wrong — and he is indifferent to whether the law is changed, for his concern is with the integrity of his own moral commitments rather than with legislative reform. The first two conditions are met, but the third fails. Yet his act is, by any reasonable assessment, a paradigmatic instance of civil disobedience.
abductive_insight: The background cases — tax refusal, labour regulation defiance, zoning ordinance noncompliance — were all instances in which the protesters aimed specifically at the repeal or amendment of the law they violated. The definition abstracted this legislative aim as essential to civil disobedience. But the background cases were drawn from a class of strategically oriented protests directed at particular policies. Civil disobedience may also be motivated by the desire to bear witness to a moral principle, without any expectation or even desire that the law in question be changed. The conscientious objector who refuses conscription as a matter of moral conviction, indifferent to legislative outcomes, is engaged in civil disobedience no less than the tax protester who seeks repeal. The definition has mistaken a common motive for the essential one, when in truth civil disobedience encompasses both strategic and testimonial forms.
passage:
Consider three cases of protest. A group of citizens refuses to pay a tax they regard as unjust, publicly announces its refusal, and accepts the penalties the courts impose; its aim is the repeal of the offending tax. Workers in a factory refuse to comply with a labour regulation they consider oppressive, openly declaring their reasons and submitting to arrest; they seek the amendment of the statute in question. Residents of a district defy a zoning ordinance they believe unjust, accepting the fines imposed; they wish the ordinance to be repealed. From these cases one might define civil disobedience as a deliberate and public refusal to comply with a specific law, accompanied by acceptance of the legal penalties, undertaken with the aim of bringing about the repeal or amendment of that law.

But consider a person who receives a conscription order directing him to serve in a war. He believes, as a matter of deep moral conviction, that the taking of human life in war is always wrong. He publicly refuses to report for service and accepts the imprisonment that follows. But his aim is not to bring about the repeal of the conscription statute. He is not a political strategist seeking legislative reform. He is a person of conscience who refuses to participate in what he regards as a moral evil, and he would refuse regardless of whether any law were changed as a result. His concern is with the integrity of his own moral commitments, not with the content of the statute book. The third condition of the definition fails: his aim is not the repeal or amendment of the conscription law. Yet his act bears every hallmark of civil disobedience — it is deliberate, public, nonviolent, and accompanied by a willingness to accept legal punishment. The definition, drawn from cases in which protesters sought specific legislative outcomes, has elevated one possible motive for civil disobedience to the status of a necessary condition, thereby excluding the testimonial form of conscientious refusal that is, for many, the most morally serious form of the practice.

background_cases: The right to vote was established by constitutional amendment after a protracted political campaign; once codified, it was enforceable in the courts.
The right to own property was recognised in the common law and subsequently codified in statute; its existence is attested by centuries of legal precedent.
The right to a fair trial was enshrined in a bill of rights adopted by the legislature; its violation may be challenged before an independent judiciary.
definition: A person has a right if and only if (i) the right is recognised in the positive law of the state, (ii) the person can invoke judicial mechanisms to enforce it, and (iii) the right's existence is attested by legal precedent or statutory text.
conditions: the right is recognised in positive law
the person can invoke judicial mechanisms to enforce it
the right's existence is attested by legal precedent or statutory text
counterexample: Consider a people living under a colonial power that recognises no right of the colonised population to self-determination. No statute or precedent of the colonial legal system acknowledges such a right, and no judicial mechanism exists through which the colonised might enforce it. All three conditions fail. Yet the claim that a people has a right to govern itself, to be free of alien domination, seems to possess a force that does not depend upon whether any legal system has seen fit to recognise it. The right, if it exists, exists prior to and independently of its legal codification.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the right to vote, the right to property, the right to a fair trial — were all instances in which rights had been successfully recognised in positive law, codified in statutes or constitutions, and rendered enforceable through judicial mechanisms. The definition abstracted legal recognition, judicial enforceability, and textual attestation as the conditions for having a right. But the background cases were drawn from settled legal orders in which rights had already won recognition. A people's right to self-determination may exist even when no legal system acknowledges it, for the moral claim of a people to govern itself does not depend upon the willingness of an occupying power to codify that claim. The definition has identified the conditions under which a right is legally effective but has confused legal effectiveness with moral existence, overlooking the possibility that rights may exist prior to and independently of their recognition in positive law.
passage:
Consider three rights whose existence is well established. The right to vote was secured by constitutional amendment after years of political agitation; once codified, it became enforceable in the courts, and any infringement could be challenged before a judge. The right to own property has been recognised in the common law for centuries and subsequently codified in statute; its existence is attested by an unbroken chain of legal precedent. The right to a fair trial was enshrined in a bill of rights adopted by the legislature and is enforceable through an independent judiciary. From these cases one might conclude that a person has a right if and only if the right is recognised in the positive law of the state, the person can invoke judicial mechanisms to enforce it, and the right's existence is attested by legal precedent or statutory text.

But consider a people living under colonial domination. The colonial power has enacted no statute recognising the right of the colonised population to govern itself. No precedent in the colonial legal system acknowledges such a right. No judicial mechanism exists through which the colonised might seek its enforcement; the courts are instruments of the colonial administration and would dismiss any such claim as without legal foundation. Every condition of the definition fails. Yet the claim that a people has a right to self-determination — a right to be free of alien rule, to govern its own affairs, to determine its own political destiny — seems to possess a moral force that is quite independent of whether any legal system has chosen to recognise it. The colonised people's right to self-governance does not spring into existence at the moment a statute acknowledges it; rather, the statute, when it comes, acknowledges something that was already there. The background cases were all instances of rights that had already secured legal recognition, and the definition mistook the signs of a right's legal effectiveness for the conditions of its existence. Rights, it would appear, may exist in a moral sense prior to and independently of their codification, and any definition that confines rights to the positive law cannot account for the moral claims that drive the very movements by which rights come to be legally recognised.

background_cases: The Treaty of Westphalia established that each prince would determine the religion of his territory, recognising the principle that sovereign authority extends to all matters within a state's borders.
The Congress of Vienna affirmed the territorial integrity and sovereign independence of the European states, treating sovereignty as the exclusive authority of each state over its own affairs.

The Monroe Doctrine asserted that the affairs of the Western Hemisphere were not to be interfered with by European powers, implicitly affirming the sovereignty of the American states over their own territories.
definition: A state is sovereign if and only if (i) it exercises exclusive authority over all matters within its borders, (ii) no other state may rightfully interfere in its internal affairs, and (iii) its territorial integrity is recognised by other states.
conditions: it exercises exclusive authority over all matters within its borders
no other state may rightfully interfere in its internal affairs
its territorial integrity is recognised by other states
counterexample: Consider a state that exercises exclusive authority within its borders, whose territorial integrity is recognised by all neighbouring states, and into whose internal affairs no foreign power interferes. Yet this state is engaged in the systematic massacre of a portion of its own population. Other states, witnessing the atrocity, argue that they are morally entitled — perhaps even morally obligated — to intervene. If the second condition is taken strictly, no intervention is permissible, for no other state may rightfully interfere. Yet the moral case for intervention seems overwhelming, and many would argue that sovereignty cannot serve as a shield behind which a government commits atrocities against its own people.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Peace of Westphalia, the Congress of Vienna, the Monroe Doctrine — were all instances in which sovereignty was invoked to protect states from external interference in an era when the principal threat to a state's autonomy was the aggression or meddling of other states. The definition abstracted non-interference and exclusive internal authority as absolute conditions of sovereignty. But the background cases did not contemplate situations in which a sovereign state would turn its power against its own population in the most extreme fashion. When a state commits mass atrocities against its own people, the principle of non-interference collides with the moral imperative to protect the innocent, and sovereignty, conceived as an absolute bar to intervention, becomes a licence for barbarity. Sovereignty, it appears, is not unconditional but is limited by the state's obligation to its own population, and when that obligation is catastrophically violated, the shield of sovereignty may be forfeit.
passage:
Consider three landmarks in the development of the concept of sovereignty. The Treaty of Westphalia established that each prince would determine the religion of his territory, recognising the principle that a sovereign's authority within his borders was not to be challenged by external powers. The Congress of Vienna reaffirmed the territorial integrity and sovereign independence of the European states, treating sovereignty as each state's exclusive dominion over its own affairs. The Monroe Doctrine asserted that the affairs of the Western Hemisphere were not to be interfered with by European powers, extending the principle of non-interference to an entire continent. From these cases one might define sovereignty as follows: a state is sovereign if and only if it exercises exclusive authority over all matters within its borders, no other state may rightfully interfere in its internal affairs, and its territorial integrity is recognised by other states.

But consider a state that satisfies all three conditions. It exercises exclusive authority within its borders; its territorial integrity is recognised by its neighbours; and no foreign power interferes in its affairs. Yet this state is engaged in the systematic massacre of a substantial portion of its own population — men, women, and children are being killed by the state's own military forces, not in the course of any armed conflict but as a deliberate policy of extermination. Other states, learning of these atrocities, consider whether they are entitled to intervene. If sovereignty is defined as the definition proposes, the answer is no: no other state may rightfully interfere in the internal affairs of a sovereign state. But the moral case for intervention is overwhelming. A principle of non-interference that forbids the international community from acting to prevent mass atrocities has, one might argue, ceased to serve the purpose for which sovereignty was conceived. The background cases all involved the protection of states from the depredations of other states, and the principle of non-interference was a bulwark against foreign aggression. None contemplated the possibility that a state would turn its sovereign power against its own people in the most extreme conceivable fashion. Sovereignty, it would seem, is not an unconditional licence but a trust, and when a government catastrophically betrays that trust, the claim to non-interference may be forfeit.

background_cases: The authority of the captain of a ship at sea, whose commands are obeyed because the safety of all aboard depends upon a single directing will, and whose competence in seamanship gives him a recognised right to command.
The authority of a judge in a court of law, whose rulings are obeyed because the administration of justice requires that disputed questions be settled by a person of recognised legal learning.

The authority of a master craftsman over his apprentices, whose instructions are followed because the apprentices recognise that the master possesses the skill and knowledge that they seek to acquire.
definition: A person X possesses authority over a group G if and only if (i) X possesses a specialised competence or expertise that the members of G lack, (ii) obedience to X's directives serves the interests of the members of G by enabling them to achieve ends that they could not achieve without X's guidance, and (iii) the authority of X is limited to the domain of X's competence and does not extend beyond it.
conditions: X possesses a specialised competence or expertise that the members of G lack
obedience to X's directives serves the interests of the members of G
the authority of X is limited to the domain of X's competence and does not extend beyond it
counterexample: Consider the authority of a hereditary monarch in a traditional kingdom. The monarch may possess no specialised competence that his subjects lack; he may be a person of ordinary abilities who inherited his throne by accident of birth. Obedience to his commands may not serve the interests of his subjects in any instrumental sense; they obey not because his guidance enables them to achieve their ends but because they regard the throne as sanctified by tradition, by religious consecration, and by the accumulated weight of centuries of custom. And his authority is not limited to any particular domain of competence; it extends, in principle, to all aspects of the life of the realm. Yet the monarch possesses genuine authority — his subjects obey him willingly, and they would regard a challenge to his authority as an offence against the natural order of things.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of authority grounded in competence — the captain, the judge, the master craftsman — in which the authority-holder possessed expertise that the governed lacked, obedience served the interests of the governed, and authority was limited to the domain of expertise. The definition abstracted competence-based, interest-serving, domain-limited authority as the essential form. But the cases were drawn from functional, expertise-based relationships, and the definition mistook the features of rational-instrumental authority for the features of authority as such. The hereditary monarch reveals that authority may rest upon tradition, custom, and religious consecration rather than upon competence; that obedience may be rendered not because it serves the interests of the governed but because the governed recognise the authority as legitimate on grounds that have nothing to do with instrumental benefit; and that authority may extend across all domains of life rather than being confined to a specific competence. The definition confused one species of authority — the authority of the expert — with the genus.
passage:
Consider three persons who plainly possess authority. The captain of a merchant vessel at sea, whose commands are obeyed without question by his crew — not because he threatens them with punishment but because the safety of the ship and all aboard depends upon a single directing will, and because the captain's long experience and seamanship give him a recognised right to command. A judge in a court of law, whose rulings settle disputes that the parties cannot settle themselves — not because the judge possesses coercive power but because the administration of justice requires that questions of law be determined by a person of recognised legal learning. A master cabinet-maker, whose instructions are followed by his apprentices — not because he compels them but because they recognise in his skill and knowledge the very thing they have come to learn. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person possesses authority over a group if and only if he possesses a specialised competence that the members of the group lack, obedience to his directives serves the interests of the group by enabling them to achieve ends they could not otherwise achieve, and his authority is limited to the domain of his competence.

But one must consider the hereditary monarch. In a traditional kingdom — let us think of the European monarchies of the early modern period — the king exercises authority over his subjects by virtue not of any specialised competence but of his birth. He may be a person of perfectly ordinary abilities; he may lack the seamanship of the captain, the legal learning of the judge, and the manual skill of the craftsman. His subjects do not obey him because his guidance enables them to achieve their ends; the peasant farmer does not need the king's expertise in order to plough his field or bring in his harvest. They obey him because they regard his authority as legitimate on grounds that have nothing to do with competence — because the throne has been occupied by his family for centuries, because the coronation ceremony has invested him with a sacred character, because custom and tradition and the weight of inherited belief tell them that it is right and natural that a king should rule. And his authority is not limited to any particular domain; it extends, in principle, over every aspect of the life of the realm — over justice, over taxation, over war and peace, over the regulation of trade and the governance of the Church. The definition, drawn from cases of competence-based authority in specialised domains, mistook the features of that species of authority for the necessary conditions of authority as such, and failed to accommodate the vast historical reality of traditional authority, which rests not upon expertise but upon custom, consecration, and the accumulated legitimacy of long-established rule.

background_cases: Two citizens of ancient Athens, both free-born adult males, who possess equal rights to speak in the assembly, to vote on legislation, to hold public office, and to serve on juries — exercising these rights in the same public forum and under the same procedural rules.

Two members of a mediaeval guild — both master craftsmen of the same trade — who possess equal standing within the guild, equal voice in its deliberations, and equal entitlement to its privileges and protections.
Two officers of equal rank in the British Army, who are subject to the same regulations, entitled to the same pay and allowances, and treated by the military hierarchy in precisely the same manner.
definition: Two persons A and B are equal if and only if (i) A and B occupy the same formally defined status or rank within a shared institutional framework, (ii) A and B possess the same entitlements and are subject to the same obligations within that framework, and (iii) A and B are interchangeable within the framework — either could fill the role occupied by the other without any alteration in the functioning of the institution.
conditions: A and B occupy the same formally defined status or rank within a shared institutional framework
A and B possess the same entitlements and are subject to the same obligations within that framework
A and B are interchangeable within the framework
counterexample: Consider a peasant farmer and a nobleman in eighteenth-century France, standing before a revolutionary tribunal that has declared all men equal. They do not occupy the same formally defined status; one was born a peasant and the other a noble, and no decree can erase the social distance between them. They are not interchangeable; the peasant cannot fill the nobleman's role in society, nor the nobleman the peasant's. Yet the tribunal declares them equal, and the equality it asserts is genuine — an equality of moral standing, of dignity, of entitlement to justice, that obtains independently of any shared institutional framework and independently of any interchangeability of social function.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of equality within formal institutional frameworks — the Athenian assembly, the mediaeval guild, the military hierarchy — in which equality was constituted by shared status, shared entitlements, and functional interchangeability. The definition abstracted these features as essential to equality. But the cases were drawn from institutions in which equality was already formally constituted, and the definition mistook institutional equality for equality as such. The revolutionary tribunal's declaration of equality between peasant and nobleman reveals that the most fundamental form of equality — equality of moral standing and human dignity — obtains independently of any shared institutional framework, independently of shared status, and independently of interchangeability. The definition confused the formal expression of equality within institutions with the moral reality that underlies and justifies those formal expressions.
passage:
Consider three pairs of persons who are plainly equal. Two citizens of ancient Athens — both free-born adult males — who possess equal rights to speak in the assembly on the Pnyx, to vote upon the measures proposed, to hold public office by lot, and to serve on the juries that try their fellow citizens. They exercise these rights in the same public forum, under the same procedural rules, and each could fill any role that the other fills. Two master craftsmen of the same guild in a mediaeval city — both admitted to the freedom of the guild, both entitled to practise their trade, both possessed of an equal voice in the guild's deliberations and an equal claim upon its privileges. Two officers of equal rank in the British Army, subject to the same regulations, entitled to the same pay and allowances, and treated by the hierarchy above and below them in precisely the same manner. From these cases one might extract a definition: two persons are equal if and only if they occupy the same formally defined status within a shared institutional framework, possess the same entitlements and obligations within that framework, and are interchangeable — either could fill the role occupied by the other.

But one must consider the revolutionary tribunal. In the France of 1789, a peasant farmer and a nobleman are brought before a tribunal that has declared, in the name of the nation, that all men are equal. The peasant and the nobleman do not occupy the same formally defined status; the entire social order of the ancien régime has been built upon the distinction between them. They do not possess the same entitlements; the nobleman has enjoyed privileges — exemption from certain taxes, precedence at court, the right to wear a sword — that the peasant has never possessed. And they are not interchangeable; the peasant cannot fill the nobleman's role in the old society, nor the nobleman the peasant's. Yet the tribunal declares them equal, and the equality it asserts is not a polite fiction or a legal convenience. It is an equality of moral standing — a declaration that the peasant and the nobleman are, as human beings, possessed of the same dignity, entitled to the same justice, and answerable to the same law. This equality obtains not within a shared institutional framework but prior to and independently of any such framework; it is the equality that the new institutions are being created to express and to protect. The definition, drawn from cases of equality within existing institutions, mistook the formal, institutional expression of equality for equality itself, and failed to accommodate the most fundamental form of equality — the equality of moral standing and human dignity that the revolutionary tradition has asserted to be the birthright of every human being, regardless of rank, station, or social function.

background_cases: The right of an Englishman not to be detained without charge, secured by the writ of habeas corpus and enforceable through the courts of common law since at least the seventeenth century.
The right of a citizen of the Swiss Confederation to participate in cantonal referenda, established by constitutional provision and protected by the federal judiciary.
The right of a member of a mediaeval guild to practise his trade within the city walls, granted by the guild's charter and enforced by the guild's own courts and officers.
definition: A person P possesses a right R if and only if (i) R was created by a specific historical act — a charter, a statute, a constitutional provision, or a judicial decision — whose date and circumstances can be identified, (ii) R is enforceable through an existing institutional mechanism whose procedures are known and accessible, and (iii) R can be forfeited or surrendered by P through specific acts or omissions defined by law.
conditions: R was created by a specific historical act whose date and circumstances can be identified
R is enforceable through an existing institutional mechanism
