R can be forfeited or surrendered by P through specific acts or omissions defined by law
counterexample: Consider the right of a person not to be subjected to torture. This right was not created by any specific historical act; it is not the product of a particular charter or statute. It is recognised, rather, as a right that all human beings possess by virtue of their humanity — a right that existed before any legislature enacted it and that would continue to exist even if every legislature in the world were to repeal its prohibition of torture. It cannot be forfeited; no act or omission by the right-holder can deprive him of the right not to be tortured. Yet it is a right, and its violation is among the gravest wrongs that one human being can inflict upon another.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of rights created by specific historical acts — habeas corpus, a constitutional provision, a guild charter — and enforceable through identifiable institutional mechanisms. The definition abstracted historical origin, institutional enforceability, and forfeitability as essential features of rights. But the cases were drawn from the domain of positive, legally constituted rights, and the definition mistook the properties of positive rights for the properties of rights as such. The right not to be tortured reveals that there are rights whose origin is not traceable to any specific historical act, whose binding force does not depend upon the existence of an enforcement mechanism, and whose possession cannot be forfeited by any act of the right-holder. The definition confused the species — positive, legally created rights — with the genus, and thereby excluded the moral rights that many regard as the most fundamental rights of all.
passage:
Consider three rights that are plainly possessed by specific persons. The right of an Englishman not to be detained without charge, secured by the ancient writ of habeas corpus — a right whose historical origins can be traced through a series of statutes and judicial decisions stretching back to at least the seventeenth century, enforceable through the courts of common law by a procedure that any lawyer can describe, and forfeitable in specified circumstances such as lawful conviction for a criminal offence. The right of a Swiss citizen to participate in cantonal referenda, established by a specific constitutional provision, protected by the federal judiciary, and subject to forfeiture in cases of legal incapacity or criminal conviction. The right of a member of a mediaeval guild to practise his trade within the city walls, granted by a charter whose date and terms are recorded, enforced by the guild's own courts, and forfeitable if the member violates the guild's ordinances. From these cases one might extract a definition: a person possesses a right if and only if the right was created by a specific historical act whose date and circumstances can be identified, is enforceable through an existing institutional mechanism, and can be forfeited through specific acts or omissions defined by law.

But one must consider the right not to be subjected to torture. This right was not created by any specific historical act. One cannot point to a charter, a statute, or a judicial decision and say: here is where the right not to be tortured came into existence. The right is recognised, rather, as a right that all human beings possess by virtue of their humanity — a right that existed before any legislature enacted a prohibition of torture, that was violated for centuries before any court acknowledged it, and that would continue to exist even if every positive law against torture were to be repealed. It does not depend for its binding force upon the existence of an institutional enforcement mechanism; it binds even in circumstances where no court exists to enforce it and no constable can be summoned to protect the person who invokes it. And it cannot be forfeited. No act or omission by the right-holder — no crime he may commit, no obligation he may violate — can deprive him of the right not to be tortured. The right is inalienable in the strictest sense; it adheres to the person as a human being, not as a citizen or a guild member or a party to any legal arrangement. The definition, drawn from cases of positive, historically created, institutionally enforced rights, mistook the features of that species of right for the features of rights as such, and thereby excluded the moral rights that constitute, for many thoughtful persons, the most fundamental and the most important rights that human beings possess.

background_cases: The sovereignty of France under Louis XIV, in which the king exercised supreme legislative, executive, and judicial authority over a unified territorial state with clearly defined borders, a single national army, and a centralised administrative apparatus.

The sovereignty of the British Crown in Parliament, in which supreme legislative authority is vested in the King-in-Parliament and no external power may override an Act of Parliament within the realm.

The sovereignty of the Ottoman Empire under Suleiman the Magnificent, in which the Sultan exercised supreme authority over a vast territorial domain, commanding a unified military, a centralised bureaucracy, and a system of law that reached from the Danube to the Nile.
definition: A political entity E is sovereign if and only if (i) supreme authority in E is concentrated in a single person or body that is clearly identifiable, (ii) E possesses a centralised administrative apparatus capable of enforcing the decisions of the supreme authority throughout its territory, and (iii) the territory of E is contiguous and its borders are clearly defined and effectively controlled.
conditions: supreme authority in E is concentrated in a single person or body that is clearly identifiable
E possesses a centralised administrative apparatus capable of enforcing the decisions of the supreme authority throughout its territory
the territory of E is contiguous and its borders are clearly defined and effectively controlled
counterexample: Consider the Holy Roman Empire in the high mediaeval period. Supreme authority was not concentrated in a single identifiable person or body; it was dispersed among the Emperor, the princes, the free cities, the bishops, and the Diet, none of whom possessed supreme authority over the whole. The Empire did not possess a centralised administrative apparatus; its governance was a patchwork of local jurisdictions, feudal obligations, and customary arrangements that varied from region to region. Its territory was not contiguous in any simple sense; it included exclaves, enclaves, and territories whose boundaries were contested and shifting. Yet the Holy Roman Empire was a sovereign entity — it was recognised as such by other states, it waged war and made peace, it sent and received ambassadors, and it exercised a species of authority, however diffuse, over its constituent parts.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all centralised, territorially consolidated sovereign states — Bourbon France, the British Crown in Parliament, the Ottoman Empire — in which sovereignty was concentrated, administered through a central apparatus, and exercised over a contiguous territory with defined borders. The definition abstracted these features as essential to sovereignty. But the cases were drawn from the domain of the centralised territorial state, which is a historically specific form of political organisation, and the definition mistook its features for the necessary conditions of sovereignty as such. The Holy Roman Empire reveals that sovereignty may be dispersed rather than concentrated, may be exercised through a patchwork of local arrangements rather than through a centralised apparatus, and may extend over a territory that is neither contiguous nor clearly bounded. The definition confused the characteristics of one historically dominant form of sovereign organisation with the essence of sovereignty itself.
passage:
Consider three political entities that are acknowledged to be sovereign. France under Louis XIV, in which the king exercised supreme legislative, executive, and judicial authority over a unified territorial state. The royal will was enforced throughout the realm by a centralised administrative apparatus — the intendants, the tax collectors, the standing army — and the borders of the kingdom were clearly defined and effectively controlled. The British Crown in Parliament, in which supreme legislative authority is vested in the King-in-Parliament, an identifiable and unified sovereign body whose enactments no external power may override. The Ottoman Empire under Suleiman, in which the Sultan exercised supreme authority over a vast domain, commanding a centralised bureaucracy, a unified military establishment, and a system of law that reached from Budapest to Cairo. From these cases one might extract a definition: a political entity is sovereign if and only if supreme authority is concentrated in a single identifiable person or body, the entity possesses a centralised administrative apparatus capable of enforcing decisions throughout its territory, and its territory is contiguous with clearly defined and effectively controlled borders.

But one must consider the Holy Roman Empire. In the twelfth and thirteenth centuries, the Holy Roman Empire was recognised by all the states of Christendom as a sovereign entity. It sent and received ambassadors. It waged war and concluded treaties. It claimed — and to some degree exercised — authority over a vast swath of central Europe. Yet supreme authority was not concentrated in a single identifiable person or body. The Emperor possessed certain prerogatives, but his authority was checked and limited at every turn by the princes, the free cities, the prince-bishops, and the Diet. No one of these bodies possessed supreme authority over the whole; sovereignty, in so far as it existed, was dispersed among a multiplicity of competing jurisdictions. The Empire did not possess a centralised administrative apparatus. There were no imperial intendants, no imperial tax collectors enforcing the Emperor's will in every province. Governance was a patchwork of local jurisdictions, feudal obligations, ecclesiastical immunities, and customary arrangements that varied enormously from one region to the next. And the territory of the Empire was not contiguous in any simple sense; it included exclaves, enclaves, and disputed marches whose boundaries shifted with every generation. The definition, drawn from cases of centralised, territorially consolidated sovereign states, mistook the features of that historically specific form of political organisation for the necessary conditions of sovereignty, and thereby excluded a political entity that was sovereign for centuries and whose sovereignty was acknowledged throughout the civilised world.

background_cases: In classical Athens, citizens gathered in the agora and decided matters of war, taxation, and public works by open vote; the assembly's decisions were binding, and the system is universally regarded as democratic.

In certain Swiss cantons, all citizens assemble annually to vote on legislation by a show of hands; the majority's decision has the force of law, and the practice is considered an exemplary form of democracy.

In the New England town meeting, residents deliberate in person upon matters of local governance — budgets, ordinances, the appointment of officials — and the majority's vote determines the outcome; this is held to be a pure form of democratic self-government.
definition: A political system is democratic if and only if (i) decisions are reached by the assembled citizens voting in person, (ii) every citizen who is present has an equal voice in the deliberation, and (iii) the decision of the assembly binds the entire community.
conditions: decisions are reached by the assembled citizens voting in person
every citizen present has an equal voice in the deliberation
the decision of the assembly binds the entire community
counterexample: Consider a modern nation of tens of millions of inhabitants. Its citizens do not assemble in any one place to vote on legislation; physical assembly is an impossibility for a polity of such size. Instead, citizens vote periodically to elect representatives, and those representatives deliberate and legislate on the citizens' behalf. The first condition fails — decisions are not reached by assembled citizens voting in person. Yet the nation is universally regarded as a democracy, and indeed as a more developed and institutionally sophisticated form of democracy than the small-scale assemblies from which the definition was drawn.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all small polities — a city-state, rural cantons, country townships — in which it was physically possible for the entire citizen body to gather in one place. The definition abstracted the fact of personal assembly as an essential feature of democracy, because in every case the citizens did literally assemble and vote. But this feature was a consequence of the small size of the polities examined, not a necessary property of democracy itself. In large nation-states, democracy is realised through representation rather than personal assembly, and the essential principle — that governmental authority derives from the will of the governed — is preserved without any literal gathering of the citizenry.
passage:
Consider three instances of democratic governance that have long served as touchstones for political thought. In classical Athens, the citizen body assembled in the agora to deliberate and vote upon matters of the highest importance — war and peace, taxation, the construction of public works. Every citizen present had an equal voice, and the assembly's decisions were binding upon the whole city. In certain Swiss cantons, citizens gather annually in an open field to vote on legislation by a show of hands, each man counting equally, and the majority's verdict has the force of law. In the New England town meeting, all residents of the community assemble to debate local matters — budgets, ordinances, appointments — and the majority's decision settles the question. From these cases one might extract a definition: a political system is democratic if and only if decisions are reached by the assembled citizens voting in person, every citizen present has an equal voice, and the decision of the assembly binds the entire community.

But consider a modern nation-state of fifty or a hundred million inhabitants. No agora could hold them; no field could accommodate them; no meeting hall could contain even a fraction of their number. The citizens of such a state do not assemble to vote on legislation. They vote, at intervals of several years, to elect representatives, and it is these representatives who assemble in a legislative chamber to deliberate and enact laws. The first condition of the definition is plainly not satisfied. Yet the state is a democracy — not merely in popular estimation but by the considered judgement of political philosophers, constitutional theorists, and the citizens themselves. The background cases were drawn from small polities in which personal assembly was feasible, and the definition abstracted this feasibility into a requirement. What the definition captured was not the essence of democracy but the form it takes in communities small enough for face-to-face governance. The essential principle of democracy — that governmental authority is derived from and accountable to the governed — can be realised through representation as well as through personal assembly, and any definition that excludes representative government has mistaken a historical accident for a conceptual necessity.

background_cases: A prisoner is confined to a cell by locked doors and armed guards; he is not free, for he is physically prevented from leaving by an external agent.
A serf is bound to his lord's estate by law; he may not leave without permission, and the legal prohibition deprives him of freedom of movement.
A citizen living under a dictatorship is forbidden by law from criticising the government; the legal prohibition, enforced by the secret police, restricts his freedom of speech.
definition: A person P is free with respect to action A if and only if (i) no physical barrier prevents P from performing A, (ii) no law prohibits P from performing A, and (iii) no agent threatens P with punishment for performing A.
conditions: no physical barrier prevents P from performing A
no law prohibits P from performing A
no agent threatens P with punishment for performing A
counterexample: Consider a person who has grown up in conditions of such extreme poverty that he has never received any education. He cannot read, he possesses no skills, and he has no knowledge of the opportunities that exist beyond his immediate circumstances. No physical barrier prevents him from entering a university; no law prohibits him from doing so; no one threatens him with punishment should he attempt it. All three conditions are satisfied with respect to the action of enrolling in a university. Yet one would hesitate to call him free to pursue higher education. He lacks the knowledge, the resources, and the capacities that would make this nominal freedom a real possibility. His poverty has constrained his freedom as effectively as any wall or statute.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved identifiable external agents or institutions that imposed constraints — a prison, a feudal law, a dictatorship's secret police. The definition abstracted freedom as the absence of such external impositions. But the case of the impoverished and uneducated man reveals that freedom may be constrained by the absence of enabling conditions — education, resources, knowledge — even when no identifiable agent or institution stands in the way. The background cases were all instances of active interference, and the definition missed the possibility that the lack of material and cognitive prerequisites can be as effective a constraint upon freedom as any wall or law.
passage:
Consider three cases in which a person's freedom is plainly curtailed. A prisoner is confined to his cell by walls, locks, and armed guards; no one would deny that he is unfree with respect to leaving the prison. A serf is bound to his lord's estate by the laws of the feudal order; he may not depart without permission, and the law is enforced by the lord's officers. A citizen under a dictatorship is forbidden by statute from criticising the government; the secret police enforce the prohibition with surveillance and imprisonment. In each case, an identifiable external constraint — physical, legal, or coercive — prevents the person from acting as he would wish. From these cases one might abstract a definition: a person is free with respect to an action if and only if no physical barrier prevents him from performing it, no law prohibits it, and no agent threatens him with punishment for performing it.

But consider a person who has spent his entire life in conditions of extreme poverty. He has never attended school; he cannot read; he possesses no trade or skill; he has no knowledge of the wider world or of the opportunities it might afford. There is no physical barrier between him and the door of a university — the campus is open, the gate is unlocked. No law forbids him from enrolling. No one threatens him with punishment should he present himself for admission. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied with respect to the action of pursuing higher education. Yet to say that this man is free to attend university seems to strain the concept of freedom beyond recognition. He does not know that the university exists, he could not understand its entrance requirements if he did, and he possesses none of the intellectual or material resources that would make enrolment a genuine possibility. The background cases were all instances of active, identifiable external interference — incarceration, legal prohibition, coercive threat — and the definition captured freedom as the absence of such interference. But the impoverished man's unfreedom does not consist in anyone's interfering with him; it consists in his lacking the capacities and resources that would render his formal liberty effective.

background_cases: In a feudal society, the nobility enjoy legal privileges denied to commoners — the right to bear arms, exemption from certain taxes, priority in the courts; the society is manifestly unequal.

In a caste system, a person's occupation, marriage prospects, and social standing are determined by birth; those born into lower castes are legally barred from the professions and honours open to higher castes; the society is manifestly unequal.

Under a system of racial segregation, one group is prohibited by law from attending the same schools, using the same facilities, or holding the same positions as another; the society is manifestly unequal.
definition: A society achieves equality if and only if (i) no person is subject to legal disabilities or privileges on account of birth, caste, or race, (ii) all persons are equal before the law — that is, the same laws apply to all, and (iii) all persons have the legal right to pursue any occupation or office.
conditions: no person is subject to legal disabilities or privileges on account of birth, caste, or race
all persons are equal before the law
all persons have the legal right to pursue any occupation or office
counterexample: Consider a society in which all legal distinctions of birth, caste, and race have been abolished. The same laws apply to every citizen; the courts make no distinction between rich and poor, noble and commoner. Every person has the legal right to pursue any occupation, enter any profession, and stand for any public office. Yet a small minority controls the overwhelming preponderance of wealth, land, and productive capital. The children of this minority receive excellent private education, form connexions with persons of influence from an early age, and enter the professions and public life with every advantage. The children of the majority attend underfunded public schools, begin labouring in youth, and have neither the resources nor the connexions to compete for positions that are in principle open to all. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied, yet the society is profoundly unequal in any substantive sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all instances of legally enforced inequality — feudal privilege, caste, racial segregation — in which the source of inequality was a formal legal distinction. The definition abstracted the removal of legal distinctions as sufficient for equality, because in every background case the inequality was constituted by law. But the case of a formally equal yet materially stratified society reveals that inequality may persist in the absence of any legal distinction, sustained by the unequal distribution of wealth, education, and social capital. The definition captured the conditions for formal equality only, and mistook the removal of legal barriers for the achievement of equality itself.
passage:
Consider three societies in which inequality is patent. In a feudal society, the nobility enjoy privileges denied to commoners: they alone may bear arms, they are exempt from certain taxes, and they receive preferential treatment in the courts. In a society organised by caste, a person's occupation and social standing are fixed at birth; those born into the lowest castes are excluded by law from the professions and honours reserved for the higher orders. Under a system of racial segregation, one group is forbidden by statute from sharing the schools, public conveyances, and civic institutions of another. In each case, inequality is constituted by a formal legal distinction — a privilege, a prohibition, a segregation enacted and enforced by the state. From these cases one might extract a definition: a society achieves equality if and only if no person is subject to legal disabilities or privileges on account of birth, caste, or race, all persons are equal before the law, and all persons have the legal right to pursue any occupation or office.

But consider a society that satisfies every one of these conditions. The law draws no distinctions of birth, caste, or race. The same statutes apply to every citizen, and the courts treat all litigants alike. Every person has the legal right to enter any profession, to own property, and to stand for public office. Yet a small class of families controls the great preponderance of the nation's wealth and productive capital. Their children attend exclusive schools, are tutored from infancy in the arts and sciences, and are introduced from an early age to persons of political and commercial influence. The children of the majority attend public schools of indifferent quality, begin working in their youth to support their families, and possess neither the money nor the connexions that would enable them to compete effectively for the positions that are nominally open to all. The society is formally equal — no legal barrier separates the classes — yet substantively unequal to a degree that the feudal order might scarcely have surpassed. The background cases were all instances in which law was the instrument of inequality, and the definition captured the removal of legal distinctions as the achievement of equality. But it overlooked the possibility that inequality may be sustained by material conditions — the distribution of wealth, education, and social capital — that operate independently of law.

background_cases: A judge issues a ruling in a court of law; the parties to the dispute comply, for the judge's authority is established by the legal system and backed by the power of the state.
A military officer commands his regiment to advance; the soldiers obey, for the officer's authority is established by the chain of command and enforced by military law.

A headmaster instructs the pupils of his school to observe a period of silence; the pupils comply, for the headmaster's authority is established by the institution of the school and recognised by both parents and pupils.
definition: An agent A has authority over a group G if and only if (i) A occupies an office or position that is recognised by a formal institution, (ii) A's commands are backed by the power of that institution to impose sanctions for non-compliance, and (iii) the members of G comply with A's commands.
conditions: A occupies an office recognised by a formal institution
A's commands are backed by institutional power to impose sanctions
the members of G comply with A's commands
counterexample: Consider an elderly woman in a traditional village who holds no official position, occupies no institutional office, and commands no sanctioning power. She is neither a magistrate nor a chieftain nor the head of any formal body. Yet when disputes arise among the villagers, they bring their grievances to her. She listens, considers, and pronounces her judgement, and the disputants accept her verdict without appeal. She exercises genuine authority — the villagers obey her not out of fear of sanctions but out of respect for her wisdom, her experience, and her known impartiality. The first and second conditions of the definition fail: she holds no institutional office and no institution backs her pronouncements with sanctions. Yet her authority is real and effective.
abductive_insight: The background cases were all drawn from formal, institutional settings — a court of law, a military hierarchy, a school — in which authority is vested in an office and backed by institutional sanctions. The definition abstracted formal institutional recognition and sanctioning power as essential to authority because these features were present in every case examined. But the village elder exercises authority without any institutional backing, solely on the basis of personal qualities — wisdom, experience, impartiality — that inspire voluntary deference. The background cases represented institutionalised authority, which is one species of authority, but not the only one. Authority may also rest upon personal charisma, moral standing, or recognised expertise, none of which requires a formal office or the power to punish.
passage:
Consider three instances of authority. A judge in a court of law issues a ruling, and the parties to the dispute comply; the judge's authority derives from the legal system, and the court possesses the power to impose penalties upon those who defy its rulings. A military officer commands his regiment to advance, and the soldiers obey; the officer's authority is established by the chain of command, and military law provides severe sanctions for disobedience. A headmaster instructs the pupils of his school to observe a period of silence, and the pupils comply; the headmaster's authority is conferred by the institution of the school, and the school possesses the power to discipline those who disregard its rules. From these cases one might extract a definition: an agent has authority over a group if and only if the agent occupies an office recognised by a formal institution, the agent's commands are backed by the institution's power to impose sanctions, and the members of the group comply.

But consider an elderly woman in a traditional village who holds no official position and occupies no institutional office. She is not a magistrate, not a chief, not the head of any council or committee. No institution stands behind her, and she possesses no power to impose sanctions of any kind upon anyone. Yet when a quarrel breaks out between two families, both parties seek her out and lay their grievances before her. She listens with patience, weighs the claims on either side, and pronounces her judgement. The disputants accept her verdict and act upon it. She exercises authority as real and as effective as that of the judge in his court, yet neither the first nor the second condition of the definition is satisfied. Her authority rests not upon institutional recognition or the threat of punishment but upon her personal qualities — her age, her wisdom, her known fairness, and the long experience of the community in finding her judgements sound. The background cases were drawn exclusively from institutional settings, and the definition abstracted institutional features as necessary to authority. But authority may arise from personal qualities that inspire voluntary deference, entirely apart from any office, institution, or power of sanction.

background_cases: A man imprisoned in a gaol is not free, for he is physically prevented from going where he wishes.
A woman forbidden by law from practising her chosen profession is not free in that respect, for the law constrains her.
A serf bound to a lord's estate and compelled to work without payment is not free, for he is subject to the coercive power of another person.

A citizen living under martial law who is forbidden to assemble with others, to publish his opinions, or to leave his city is not free, for the government has imposed severe restrictions upon his conduct.
definition: A person P is free if and only if (i) no physical barrier prevents P from performing the actions P wishes to perform, (ii) no law or regulation prohibits P from performing those actions, and (iii) no other person exercises coercive power over P — that is, no one compels P to act against P's own wishes by means of threats, force, or domination.
conditions: No physical barrier prevents P from performing desired actions
No law or regulation prohibits those actions
No other person exercises coercive power over P
counterexample: Consider a woman who lives in a society with no legal barriers to her advancement. No law prevents her from entering any profession, pursuing any education, or participating in public life. No person exercises coercive power over her — she is not threatened, forced, or dominated. No physical barrier constrains her movement. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the social customs of her community are such that a woman who attempts to enter a profession traditionally reserved for men is met with universal disapproval — she is shunned by her neighbours, her family withdraws its support, employers refuse to hire her (not because the law forbids it, but because custom dictates otherwise), and she finds that every avenue of advancement is blocked by the invisible but impenetrable wall of social convention. She is, in the practically relevant sense, not free to pursue her chosen career, though no law forbids it, no person coerces her, and no physical barrier stands in her way.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the prisoner, the woman under legal prohibition, the serf, the citizen under martial law — all involved formal, identifiable constraints: walls, laws, masters, governmental decrees. The definition abstracted formal constraint as the sole obstacle to freedom. But the woman constrained by social custom reveals that freedom may be curtailed by informal, diffuse, and uncodified social pressures that are as effective as any law or chain, even though no single person or institution can be identified as the source of the constraint.
passage:
Consider four cases in which a person is not free. A man imprisoned in a gaol cannot go where he wishes, for the walls and the locked door prevent him. A woman forbidden by law from practising medicine cannot pursue her vocation, for the statute constrains her. A serf bound to a lord's estate cannot leave or choose his own work, for his lord exercises coercive power over him. A citizen under martial law cannot assemble, publish, or travel, for the government has imposed restrictions by decree. In each case, the unfreedom has a clear, identifiable source: a physical barrier, a legal prohibition, a coercive master, a governmental order. One might extract a definition: a person is free if and only if no physical barrier, no legal prohibition, and no coercive power prevents the person from acting as the person wishes.

But consider a woman living in a society that has recently abolished all legal distinctions between the sexes. No law prevents her from entering any profession, pursuing any education, or participating in any aspect of public life. No person exercises coercive power over her; she is not threatened, not dominated, not compelled by any master. No wall or locked door stands between her and the life she wishes to lead. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied; she is, by the definition, free. Yet the social customs of her community are such that a woman who attempts to enter a profession traditionally reserved for men — the law, let us say, or medicine, or the academy — is met with a resistance that, though it takes no legal or physical form, is as effective as any barrier. Her neighbours regard her ambition with suspicion; her family withdraws its support and its affection; employers, though not forbidden by law from hiring her, decline to do so, for custom has not yet caught up with the law; and she finds, after months of effort, that every avenue of advancement is closed to her — not by any identifiable constraint, but by the cumulative weight of social expectation and disapproval. She is not free to pursue her career in any sense that matters, though the definition pronounces her free. The background cases were all cases of formal, visible, codifiable constraint — prisons, laws, masters — and the definition abstracted formal constraint as the sole enemy of freedom. The woman's case reveals that the most pervasive and effective obstacles to freedom may be informal, diffuse, and invisible, arising not from any single source but from the ambient pressure of social convention.

background_cases: In a feudal society, the lord has legal privileges that the serf does not — the right to bear arms, the right to administer justice, the right to move freely — and we say that the society is unequal.
In a caste system, persons born into the priestly caste have access to religious offices and education that are denied to those born into lower castes, and we say the society is unequal.
Under laws that restrict the franchise to property-owning men, those without property and all women are excluded from political participation, and we say the society is unequal.
definition: A society S is equal if and only if (i) no person in S possesses legal privileges that are denied to other persons on the basis of birth, caste, or hereditary rank, (ii) all persons in S have access to the same public institutions — courts, schools, offices — on the same terms, and (iii) no law in S discriminates among persons on the basis of any inherited characteristic.
conditions: No person possesses legal privileges denied to others on the basis of birth, caste, or rank
All persons have access to the same public institutions on the same terms
No law discriminates on the basis of any inherited characteristic
counterexample: Consider a society that has abolished all hereditary privilege. No law discriminates on the basis of birth or caste. Every citizen has access to the same courts, the same schools, and the same public offices on identical terms. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the society contains vast disparities of inherited wealth. The children of the rich inherit fortunes that enable them to purchase an education of the highest quality, to establish themselves in lucrative professions, and to accumulate further wealth, while the children of the poor inherit nothing and must make their way without resources, connections, or the education that would enable them to compete on equal terms. Over the generations, these disparities harden into something resembling a hereditary class system — not legally sanctioned, but practically unbreakable. The society satisfies the definition of equality, yet it is manifestly unequal.
abductive_insight: The background cases — feudalism, caste, restricted franchise — were all cases of inequality imposed and maintained by law, and the definition abstracted legal equality as the essence of equality. But the society with vast inherited wealth disparities reveals that inequality may perpetuate itself through economic mechanisms that operate independently of the law, and that legal equality, though necessary, is not sufficient to produce a genuinely equal society.
passage:
Consider three cases of what we should call inequality. In a feudal society, the lord possesses legal privileges — the right to bear arms, to administer justice, to move freely — that are denied to the serf, and the distribution of these privileges is determined by birth. In a caste system, the priestly caste has access to religious offices and education that are denied to lower castes, and one's caste is fixed by the accident of birth. Under a restricted franchise, only property-owning men may vote, and women and the propertyless are excluded from political participation. In each case, the inequality is inscribed in law and attached to an inherited characteristic — birth, caste, sex, or property. One might extract a definition: a society is equal if and only if no person possesses legal privileges denied to others on the basis of birth or rank, all persons have access to the same institutions on the same terms, and no law discriminates on the basis of inherited characteristics.

But consider a society that has, with great effort and over many generations, abolished all hereditary legal privilege. No law discriminates on the basis of birth, caste, sex, or any other inherited characteristic. Every citizen has access to the same courts, the same schools, and the same public offices on identical terms. The reforms are genuine and the legal framework is scrupulously egalitarian. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied. Yet within this legally equal society, there exist vast disparities of inherited wealth. A small number of families possess enormous fortunes, accumulated over generations and transmitted from parent to child. The children of these families attend the finest schools, are tutored by the best teachers, travel widely, form connections with others of their class, and enter adult life with every conceivable advantage. The children of the poor — equally entitled, in law, to attend those same schools — cannot do so, for the fees are beyond their means. They attend inferior schools, receive an inferior education, and enter a labour market in which they cannot compete with those who have had every advantage. Over the generations, these economic disparities harden into something that resembles a hereditary class system — not sanctioned by law, not recognised by the constitution, but practically unbreakable. The background cases were all cases of legal inequality, and the definition abstracted legal equality as the whole of equality. The society with inherited wealth reveals that inequality may reproduce itself through economic channels that the law does not reach, and that a society may be formally equal and substantively stratified at the same time.

background_cases: A king who inherits his throne commands obedience from his subjects, who recognise his authority as deriving from the established line of succession; we say the king has authority.

A president elected by popular vote issues executive orders that the civil service carries out; the citizens recognise her authority as deriving from the electoral process; we say the president has authority.

A judge appointed according to the procedures established by law delivers verdicts that the parties obey; his authority derives from the legal system that appointed him; we say the judge has authority.
definition: A person or institution A has authority if and only if (i) A occupies a recognised position within an established institutional framework — a monarchy, a republic, a legal system, (ii) A acquired the position through the procedures that the institutional framework prescribes, and (iii) the persons subject to A's authority recognise the institutional framework as legitimate.
conditions: A occupies a recognised position within an established institutional framework
A acquired the position through prescribed procedures
The persons subject to A's authority recognise the framework as legitimate
counterexample: Consider the authority exercised by a person of exceptional moral stature in a community — a figure such as Gandhi during the Indian independence movement. Gandhi held no office in any institutional framework; he was not a king, not a president, not a judge. He did not acquire his influence through any prescribed procedure. Yet millions of people deferred to his moral authority, followed his directives, and altered their conduct in accordance with his counsel. His authority was real — as real in its practical effects as that of any officeholder — yet it derived not from any institution but from the force of his character, his moral vision, and the trust that others placed in him. All three conditions of the definition fail.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the king, the president, the judge — were all figures who occupied positions within established institutional structures, and the definition abstracted institutional position as a necessary condition of authority. But the figure of exceptional moral stature reveals that authority may arise from character, wisdom, and moral standing rather than from any institutional framework, and that the most profound forms of authority may be precisely those that owe nothing to the machinery of office.
passage:
Consider three cases of what we should call authority. A king inherits his throne according to the laws of succession and commands the obedience of his subjects. A president is elected by popular vote and issues executive orders that the civil service carries out. A judge is appointed according to established legal procedures and delivers verdicts that the parties obey. In each case, the authority figure occupies a recognised position within an institutional framework, acquired the position through the procedures that framework prescribes, and is recognised by those subject to the authority as holding a legitimate office. One might extract a definition: a person has authority if and only if the person occupies a recognised institutional position, acquired it through prescribed procedures, and is recognised by those subject to the authority as legitimate.

But consider a figure of exceptional moral stature — a person such as Gandhi during the movement for Indian independence. Gandhi held no office in any governmental or institutional framework. He was not a king, not a president, not a minister, not a judge. He did not acquire his influence through any procedure prescribed by any institution — there was no election, no appointment, no succession. He was a private citizen, a barrister who had given up his practice, a man in homespun cloth who walked the roads of India. Yet millions of people deferred to his authority. When he called for a day of prayer and fasting, the nation fasted. When he called for non-cooperation with the colonial government, millions withdrew their cooperation. When he walked to the sea to make salt in defiance of the salt tax, the gesture shook an empire. His authority was as real in its practical effects — as potent in its capacity to direct the conduct of others — as that of any king or president. Yet all three conditions of the definition fail. He occupied no institutional position; he acquired his influence through no prescribed procedure; and his authority did not depend upon the recognition of any institutional framework as legitimate. The background cases were drawn from institutional contexts — monarchies, republics, legal systems — and the definition abstracted institutional position as essential to authority. Gandhi reveals that authority may arise from the force of moral character and the trust of a people, and that it may be more compelling in its exercise than any authority that rests upon the machinery of office alone.

background_cases: In a small town, all adult residents gather in a hall, debate a proposed ordinance, and decide by majority vote whether to adopt it. We regard this as a paradigmatic exercise of democratic governance.
In a modern republic, citizens choose their representatives through competitive elections held at regular intervals, with universal adult suffrage. We regard this, too, as democratic.
In a military dictatorship, the general who seized power issues decrees without consulting the population. We regard this as unambiguously undemocratic.
definition: A political system is democratic if and only if (i) political decisions are made, directly or through elected representatives, by majority vote, (ii) all adult citizens have the right to participate in voting, and (iii) elections or votes are held at regular intervals.
conditions: Political decisions are made by majority vote, directly or through representatives
All adult citizens may participate in voting
Elections or votes are held at regular intervals
counterexample: Consider a society in which elections are held at regular intervals, all adults vote, and decisions are made by majority rule. Yet the majority consistently uses its power to strip a despised ethnic minority of its civil standing — seizing its property, excluding its members from public life, and imposing increasingly harsh restrictions upon it. All three conditions of the definition are satisfied without remainder. Yet it seems deeply wrong to describe such a system as a well-functioning democracy, for it deploys democratic mechanisms to destroy the very principles — equal dignity, reciprocal respect — that democratic governance is ordinarily thought to serve.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve communities in which majority rule operates within an implicit framework of mutual respect and restraint. The definition, drawn from these benign cases, captures the procedural machinery of democracy without the normative constraints that, in the background cases, were present but unexamined.
passage:
The background cases suggest a procedural definition of democracy. The town meeting decides by majority vote with full participation; the republic elects representatives through competitive elections; the dictatorship does neither. From these cases one might propose that a political system is democratic if and only if political decisions are made by majority vote, directly or through elected representatives, all adult citizens have the right to participate, and elections or votes are held at regular intervals. The definition captures the common structure of the two democratic cases and correctly excludes the dictatorial one.

Yet the background cases share a feature that the definition does not record: in each democratic case, the majority exercises its power within a framework of implicit constraints. The town meeting does not vote to expel a family it dislikes; the republic does not use its electoral machinery to disenfranchise a minority. These constraints are so deeply embedded in the background cases that they pass without notice. A case which reveals their absence from the definition may be described as follows. Consider a society in which elections are held regularly, all adults vote, and decisions are reached by majority rule. The majority, animated by hostility toward a particular ethnic group, uses its power to confiscate the group's property, to bar its members from public office, and to restrict their freedom of movement. All three conditions of the definition are met with perfect fidelity. But the system is democratic only in the most hollow and formal sense. It deploys the procedural apparatus of democracy in the service of oppression. What the definition lacks, and what the background cases concealed, is a condition requiring that democratic procedures operate within substantive limits — protections for minorities, guarantees of basic liberties — without which majority rule becomes a vehicle for tyranny rather than self-governance.

background_cases: A man walks through an open field with no fences, no guards, and no laws restricting his movement. We say he is free to go where he pleases.
A prisoner is confined to a cell by locked doors and armed guards. We say she is unfree with respect to her movement.
A citizen may travel abroad, the borders being open and no law prohibiting departure. We say she is free to leave the country.
definition: A person P is free with respect to action A if and only if (i) no physical barrier prevents P from performing A, (ii) no law prohibits P from performing A, and (iii) no other person or institution coerces P into refraining from A.
conditions: No physical barrier prevents P from performing A
No law prohibits P from performing A
No person or institution coerces P into refraining from A
counterexample: Consider a citizen in a state where the border is open, no law prohibits departure, and no person or institution threatens her with punishment for leaving. Yet she cannot leave because she lacks the financial means to purchase a passport, pay for transport, or sustain herself abroad — and the state, while imposing no prohibition, provides no assistance. All three conditions are satisfied: there is no physical barrier at the border, no law against leaving, and no coercion. Yet she is as effectively trapped as the prisoner, her confinement achieved not by walls or laws but by destitution.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve agents for whom the absence of external impediments is sufficient for freedom because the agents possess the material means to act. The definition, drawn from these cases, identifies freedom with the absence of obstruction and overlooks the possibility that the absence of positive capacity — resources, means, opportunity — may constrain as effectively as any wall or law.
passage:
The background cases invite a purely negative conception of freedom. The man in the open field is free because nothing obstructs him; the prisoner is unfree because walls and guards confine her; the citizen may leave because the border is open and the law permits it. From these cases one might extract the definition that a person P is free with respect to action A if and only if no physical barrier prevents P from performing A, no law prohibits P from performing A, and no other person or institution coerces P into refraining from A. The definition identifies freedom with the absence of obstruction — physical, legal, and coercive.

Yet the background cases all involve agents who, once the external impediments are removed, possess the means to act. The man in the field can walk. The citizen with an open border can afford to travel. The definition, drawn from cases of this kind, inherits their hidden assumption that the agent's own capacities and resources are adequate. A case which reveals the insufficiency of this assumption is not difficult to describe. Consider a citizen who lives in a state where the border stands open, where no law forbids her departure, and where no person or institution threatens her in any way. She is, by the definition's lights, entirely free to leave. Yet she is destitute. She cannot afford a passport, a ticket, or the means to sustain herself upon arrival. She is confined as effectively as any prisoner, but by poverty rather than by walls. The definition, having been abstracted from cases in which the agent's material means were never in question, fails to recognise that freedom may require not merely the absence of external obstruction but the presence of positive capacity — the resources and opportunities without which the formal absence of barriers is an empty freedom indeed.

background_cases: A judge sentences a convicted criminal to imprisonment. The sentence is obeyed because the judge occupies an office established by law, and the legal system is regarded as legitimate by the population. We regard this as an exercise of genuine authority.

A teacher instructs a pupil to complete an assignment. The pupil complies because the teacher occupies a recognised educational role, and the school's authority is accepted by pupils and parents. We regard this as a case of authority, albeit of a more limited kind.
A bandit demands money at gunpoint. The victim complies out of fear. We do not regard the bandit as possessing authority; he has power but not the right to command.
definition: A person or body P has authority over person Q if and only if (i) P issues directives that Q is expected to follow, (ii) P's position is recognised within an institutional framework accepted by the relevant community, and (iii) Q complies with P's directives.
conditions: P issues directives Q is expected to follow
P's position is recognised within an accepted institutional framework
Q complies with P's directives
counterexample: Consider a government minister who issues a directive to a civil servant. The minister occupies a recognised position within the governmental framework, the civil servant is expected to comply, and the civil servant does in fact comply — but only because the minister has privately threatened to ruin the civil servant's career through fabricated allegations unless the directive is obeyed. The compliance is genuine, the institutional framework is in place, and the directive is issued from a position of authority. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the actual basis of compliance is personal intimidation rather than institutional authority. The minister has authority in the formal sense, but in this particular exercise he has operated not through his authority but through his private power to coerce.
abductive_insight: The background cases distinguish the judge and teacher from the bandit by the presence of institutional recognition. But they share an unexamined feature: in each case of genuine authority, the compliance is motivated by the institutional position itself rather than by extra-institutional threats. The definition captures the institutional setting but not the motivational connection between the institution and the compliance, allowing coercion dressed in institutional clothing to satisfy all three conditions.
passage:
The background cases suggest a clear criterion for distinguishing authority from mere power. The judge and the teacher operate within recognised institutional frameworks, and their directives are followed because of the positions they occupy. The bandit operates outside any such framework, and his victim's compliance is motivated by fear alone. From these cases one might propose that a person or body P has authority over person Q if and only if P issues directives that Q is expected to follow, P's position is recognised within an institutional framework accepted by the relevant community, and Q complies with P's directives.

The definition correctly distinguishes the background cases. But the background cases of genuine authority share a further feature that the definition does not capture: in each case, Q's compliance is motivated by the institutional position of P, not by extra-institutional threats. The pupil obeys the teacher because the teacher is the teacher, not because the teacher has threatened to harm the pupil outside school. The convicted criminal submits to the sentence because the judge's office carries the weight of law. The definition records the institutional setting and the fact of compliance, but not the connection between them. Consider a government minister who issues a directive to a civil servant. The minister occupies a recognised institutional position, the civil servant is expected to comply, and the civil servant does comply. But the civil servant's compliance is not motivated by the minister's institutional authority; it is motivated by a private threat — the minister has intimated that non-compliance will result in career destruction through fabricated allegations. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet the exercise of power in this case is not an exercise of authority; it is coercion operating within an institutional disguise. The definition, drawn from cases where institutional position and motivational basis coincided, fails to require that the compliance be appropriately connected to the institutional framework rather than to personal intimidation.

background_cases: In classical Athens, citizens gathered in the assembly to debate and vote directly on matters of policy — war, taxation, public works. The democratic character of the system resided in the direct participation of citizens in the decisions that affected their lives.

In the New England town meeting, residents gather in person to debate and vote on the town's budget, bylaws, and public projects. The democratic character of the system lies in the direct, unmediated involvement of citizens in governance.

In the Swiss canton of Appenzell Innerrhoden, citizens gather annually in the Landsgemeinde — the open-air assembly — to vote on cantonal legislation by a show of hands. The democratic character is expressed through the physical presence and direct vote of each citizen.
definition: A political system is democratic if and only if (i) citizens participate directly in the making of laws — they vote on legislation themselves rather than through representatives, (ii) all adult citizens are eligible to participate, and (iii) decisions are made by majority vote.
conditions: Citizens participate directly in lawmaking
All adult citizens are eligible to participate
Decisions are made by majority vote
counterexample: Consider the United Kingdom. Citizens do not vote directly on legislation; they elect members of Parliament, who then debate and enact laws on their behalf. Condition (i) is not met. Yet the United Kingdom is widely regarded as one of the oldest and most stable democracies in the world. Its democratic character resides not in the direct participation of citizens in lawmaking but in their ability to choose and hold accountable the representatives who make laws on their behalf. The definition, by requiring direct participation, excludes representative democracies — which constitute the vast majority of the world's democracies — from the category of democratic systems.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Athens, the New England town meeting, the Swiss Landsgemeinde — are all instances of direct democracy, in which citizens participate personally in legislation. The definition generalises from these cases to require direct participation as a necessary condition. But representative democracies, in which citizens govern through elected representatives rather than through personal legislative participation, reveal that the background cases were drawn from a particular and historically specific form of democracy, and the definition excludes the form of democracy that is, in the modern world, by far the most prevalent.
passage:
The historical cases that have most powerfully shaped our conception of democracy all involve direct participation. In classical Athens, citizens debated and voted on legislation in the assembly. In the New England town meeting, residents gather to decide the town's affairs by their own votes. In the Swiss Landsgemeinde, citizens assemble in the open air to enact cantonal laws. These cases suggest a definition: a political system is democratic if and only if citizens participate directly in making laws, all adult citizens are eligible to participate, and decisions are reached by majority vote.

Yet this definition, though it faithfully captures the features of the background cases, encounters a difficulty when applied to the political systems that most people today would unhesitatingly call democratic. Consider the United Kingdom. British citizens do not vote on legislation. They elect members of Parliament, who then debate, amend, and enact laws in Westminster. The citizen's role is to choose representatives and to hold them accountable at the next election, not to participate directly in the legislative process. Condition (i) — direct participation in lawmaking — is not met. The definition therefore implies that the United Kingdom is not a democracy. But the United Kingdom has been regarded as a democracy, and indeed as a model of democratic governance, for generations. The background cases, it now appears, were drawn exclusively from instances of direct democracy — a form of government that is possible only in small communities where all citizens can physically assemble. The definition has generalised from these cases to a condition — direct participation — that excludes the representative form of democracy upon which virtually every modern democratic state is built.

background_cases: A man imprisoned in a cell is unfree: the locked door physically prevents him from leaving, and his inability to leave is the direct result of another agent's deliberate action.

A woman forbidden by law from entering a profession is unfree with respect to that profession: the legal prohibition, backed by the threat of punishment, prevents her from pursuing the career she desires.
A serf bound to the land by feudal obligation is unfree: the lord's authority, enforced by custom and coercion, prevents the serf from leaving the estate or choosing an alternative occupation.
definition: A person P is unfree with respect to an action A if and only if (i) another agent deliberately prevents P from performing A, (ii) the prevention is achieved through physical force, legal prohibition, or the threat of punishment, and (iii) P desires to perform A.
conditions: Another agent deliberately prevents P from performing A
Prevention is achieved through force, legal prohibition, or threat of punishment
P desires to perform A
counterexample: Consider a woman living in a deeply conservative community where no law forbids her from pursuing higher education, no one physically prevents her from applying to a university, and no one threatens her with punishment. But the social norms of her community are so powerful, and the disapproval she would face so overwhelming, that she never considers the possibility. She has internalised the expectation that women do not pursue education, and she does not desire to do so — not because she has freely chosen against it, but because the social structure has shaped her desires so thoroughly that the option has never entered her consciousness. No agent deliberately prevents her; no force, law, or threat is deployed; and she does not desire the action. All three conditions fail. Yet one might argue that she is profoundly unfree — that her freedom has been constrained not by any identifiable act of coercion but by the invisible pressure of social norms that have shaped her very desires.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve unfreedom produced by identifiable agents using identifiable means — a jailer, a lawmaker, a feudal lord — against a subject who desires to act. The definition generalises from these conspicuous cases to require deliberate prevention by an identifiable agent. But structural unfreedom — the constraint produced by social norms, cultural expectations, and internalised limitations — operates without an identifiable preventer and may even extinguish the desire to act, rendering it invisible to a definition that requires both a deliberate agent and a thwarted desire.
passage:
The cases in which we most readily recognise unfreedom involve identifiable agents who deploy identifiable means of prevention against subjects who desire to act. The prisoner is confined by the jailer's lock. The woman is excluded from a profession by the legislator's statute. The serf is bound to the land by the lord's authority. In each case, there is an agent who prevents, a means of prevention — physical force, legal prohibition, coercive threat — and a desire on the part of the subject that is thwarted. One might, on the basis of these cases, define unfreedom as deliberate prevention by an agent, achieved through force or legal coercion, of an action the subject desires to perform.

Yet this definition, though it captures the features of the background cases, is blind to a form of unfreedom that is arguably more pervasive and more insidious than any the background cases illustrate. Consider a woman in a community where social norms so thoroughly circumscribe the roles available to her that she has never conceived of the possibility of pursuing an education. No one locks a door against her; no law forbids her; no one threatens her with punishment. The constraints upon her are not physical, legal, or coercive in any obvious sense. They are structural — embedded in the expectations, assumptions, and values of her community, transmitted through upbringing and reinforced by social pressure so subtle as to be nearly invisible. And, most significantly, these constraints have shaped her desires: she does not want what she has never been permitted to imagine. All three conditions of the definition fail. No agent deliberately prevents her; no force or legal prohibition is employed; and she does not desire the forbidden action. The definition pronounces her free. Yet her freedom has been as thoroughly curtailed as that of the prisoner or the serf — not by bars or chains but by the invisible architecture of social expectation.

background_cases: A judge has authority in the courtroom: litigants obey her rulings not merely because they fear the consequences of disobedience but because they recognise that the judge occupies a role that confers upon her the right to issue binding decisions.

A military officer has authority over her soldiers: they obey her orders not merely out of fear of court-martial but because they recognise the legitimacy of the chain of command and the officer's right, by virtue of her rank, to command.

A democratically elected prime minister has authority over the civil service: officials carry out his directives not merely because they are paid to do so but because they recognise that the electoral process confers upon the prime minister a right to direct the affairs of government.
definition: A person X has authority over a group G if and only if (i) X occupies a role that is recognised by the members of G as conferring the right to issue directives, (ii) the members of G comply with X's directives because they recognise this right, and (iii) X's role is defined and constrained by a system of rules that specifies the scope and limits of X's authority.
conditions: X occupies a role recognised as conferring the right to issue directives
Members of G comply because they recognise this right
X's role is defined and constrained by a system of rules
counterexample: Consider a figure such as Mahatma Gandhi during the Indian independence movement. Gandhi held no office defined by any system of rules. He was not a judge, a military officer, or an elected official. No formal role conferred upon him the right to issue directives. Yet millions of people obeyed his calls for boycotts, strikes, and civil resistance — not out of fear, not out of habit, but because they recognised in him a moral authority that transcended any formal role. He had authority over a vast movement, yet his authority satisfied none of the three conditions: he occupied no formally defined role, his followers' compliance was not based on recognition of a formal right, and no system of rules defined the scope of his authority.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the judge, the officer, the prime minister — all involve authority grounded in formally defined institutional roles with codified rules and recognised rights. The definition generalises from these institutional cases to require a formal role and a constraining system of rules. But charismatic and moral authority — the authority of a Gandhi or a Martin Luther King — arises outside any institutional framework, revealing that the background cases were drawn exclusively from formal institutions and the definition cannot accommodate authority that rests on personal moral standing rather than on institutional position.
passage:
The cases from which our concept of authority is most commonly derived are institutional in character. The judge's authority derives from her judicial office; the officer's authority derives from her military rank; the prime minister's authority derives from his electoral mandate. In each case, the authority figure occupies a formally defined role, the group complies because it recognises the right conferred by that role, and a system of rules specifies what the authority may and may not demand. One might, on the basis of these cases, define authority as requiring a formally defined role recognised as conferring the right to command, compliance based on recognition of that right, and a system of rules that constrains the exercise of authority.

Yet this definition, though it faithfully captures the structure of the background cases, encounters a profound difficulty with forms of authority that arise outside institutional frameworks. Consider the case of a figure such as Mahatma Gandhi during the movement for Indian independence. Gandhi held no office. No system of rules defined his role or specified the scope of his authority. He was not a judge, an officer, or an elected leader. Yet his authority was immense. When he called for a salt march, hundreds of thousands followed. When he declared a fast, the political landscape shifted. His followers obeyed not because they recognised a formal right conferred by an institutional role, but because they recognised in Gandhi a moral authority — a standing that derived from his personal character, his sacrifices, and his vision, rather than from any position in an institutional hierarchy. The definition excludes this form of authority entirely. None of its three conditions are met. The background cases, it appears, were drawn from the domain of formal institutions, and the definition cannot accommodate the charismatic and moral authority that has, throughout history, moved millions without the backing of any institutional framework.

background_cases: The Athenian assembly, in which citizens gathered to debate and vote directly on legislation, is regarded as a paradigm of democracy because the citizens themselves made the laws under which they lived, without the mediation of elected representatives.

The New England town meeting, in which all adult inhabitants of a town gather to discuss and vote on local matters, is regarded as democratic because decisions are made by the people directly assembled, not by delegates acting on their behalf.

The Swiss cantonal Landsgemeinde, in which citizens of a canton assemble in the open air to vote on legislation by show of hands, is regarded as democratic because the people govern themselves directly, debating and deciding matters of common concern without intermediaries.
definition: A political system P is a democracy if and only if (i) the citizens of P make political decisions by direct vote, (ii) every adult citizen is entitled to participate in the decision-making process, and (iii) the decisions are made by majority rule.
conditions: Citizens make political decisions by direct vote
Every adult citizen is entitled to participate
Decisions are made by majority rule
counterexample: Consider a modern nation-state with a population of many millions. The citizens do not make political decisions by direct vote; they elect representatives who deliberate and legislate on their behalf. No citizen votes directly on legislation. The first condition is not met. Yet such a system — representative government with universal suffrage, competitive elections, and an independent judiciary — is universally described as a democracy. Indeed, virtually every system commonly called a democracy in the modern world fails to satisfy the first condition.
abductive_insight: The background cases — Athens, New England, Switzerland — are all instances of direct democracy in small communities where the population is small enough to assemble in one place. The definition captures the features of these direct democracies but cannot accommodate representative democracy, which is the predominant form in the modern world. The background cases are drawn from a specific historical type, and the definition mistakes the features of that type for necessary features of democracy as such.
passage:
The political systems most readily identified as democratic by historians share a common structural feature. In the Athenian assembly, citizens gathered to debate and decide legislation by direct vote. In the New England town meeting, the inhabitants of a community assembled to determine local affairs by their own votes. In the Swiss cantonal Landsgemeinde, citizens voted on legislation by show of hands in the open air. In each case, the people governed themselves directly: there was no intermediary between the citizen and the law.

One might codify these cases by defining democracy as a system in which citizens make political decisions by direct vote, every adult citizen is entitled to participate, and decisions are made by majority rule. This definition captures the paradigm cases with precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by the modern nation-state. Consider a country of many millions of inhabitants. The citizens do not vote directly on legislation; they elect representatives — members of parliament, senators, delegates — who debate and decide on their behalf. No citizen casts a vote for or against a particular statute. The first condition of the definition is not satisfied. Yet the political system of such a state — with competitive elections, universal suffrage, an independent judiciary, and the protection of civil liberties — is universally described as a democracy. Indeed, virtually every system called a democracy in the contemporary world is representative rather than direct. The definition, built from the paradigm of small-community direct rule, mistakes the features of that particular institutional form for necessary features of democracy as such. The background cases are all instances of a single type — direct democracy — and the definition cannot accommodate the representative form that has become the standard expression of the democratic idea in the modern era.

background_cases: A prisoner released from gaol is said to have gained his freedom: the chains are removed, the doors are opened, and no one prevents him from going where he pleases. His freedom consists in the removal of physical restraint.

A serf emancipated from bondage is said to have gained freedom: the legal prohibition against his leaving the manor is abolished, and no lord may compel his labour. His freedom consists in the removal of legal compulsion.

A journalist in a state that lifts censorship is said to have gained freedom of the press: the laws prohibiting the publication of certain opinions are repealed, and no official prevents the journalist from publishing. His freedom consists in the removal of governmental restriction.
definition: An individual I is free if and only if (i) no physical restraint prevents I from acting as I chooses, (ii) no legal prohibition restricts I's actions, and (iii) no other person coerces I through threats of harm.
conditions: No physical restraint prevents I from acting
No legal prohibition restricts I's actions
No other person coerces I through threats
counterexample: Consider a woman in a society that places no physical restraint upon her, enacts no law against her working outside the home, and in which no one threatens her with harm for doing so. She is, by the definition, entirely free to pursue a career. Yet the social customs of her community are such that any woman who works outside the home is shunned by her neighbours, excluded from community life, and subjected to relentless disapproval and social pressure. No law compels her conformity; no one threatens violence; no chain binds her. Yet the social cost of exercising her nominal freedom is so severe that she cannot, in practice, bring herself to bear it. The definition declares her free, but her freedom is illusory.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the prisoner, the serf, the journalist — all involve the removal of identifiable, discrete obstacles: chains, legal bonds, censorship laws. The definition captures this removal-of-obstacles picture but fails to accommodate the more diffuse forms of constraint that operate through social norms, custom, and collective disapproval. The background cases are all cases where the constraint is formal and identifiable; the definition, built from these cases, cannot recognise constraints that are informal, pervasive, and socially rather than legally or physically imposed.
passage:
The cases in which freedom is most readily recognised all involve the removal of identifiable obstacles. The prisoner gains freedom when the chains are struck off and the cell door is opened. The emancipated serf gains freedom when the legal bonds of serfdom are dissolved. The journalist gains freedom of the press when censorship laws are repealed. In each case, the obstacle is specific, identifiable, and removable — a physical restraint, a legal prohibition, a governmental restriction — and its removal constitutes the gaining of freedom.
