One might codify these cases by defining freedom as the absence of physical restraint, legal prohibition, and personal coercion through threats. This definition captures the negative conception of liberty — freedom as the absence of interference — with admirable clarity. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when we consider the constraining power of social norms. Consider a woman living in a community in which no law prevents her from working outside the home, no physical restraint confines her, and no one threatens her with violence. She is, by the definition, perfectly free to pursue a career. But the customs of her community are such that any woman who works outside the home is ostracised: shunned by neighbours, excluded from social gatherings, subjected to relentless disapproval. The pressure is not physical, not legal, not coercive in the narrow sense of involving threats of harm. Yet it is effective. The social cost of exercising her freedom is so prohibitive that she cannot, in practice, exercise it. The background cases, from which the definition was abstracted, all involved the removal of formal, identifiable constraints. They did not encompass the informal, diffuse, yet enormously powerful constraints imposed by social custom and collective opinion. The definition, built from these cases, cannot recognise a form of unfreedom that operates without chains, without laws, and without threats.

background_cases: The authority of a judge in a court of law is recognised because the judge occupies an office established by law, has been appointed through procedures recognised as legitimate, and exercises powers defined and limited by a legal framework. Citizens comply with the judge's rulings not because they fear the judge personally but because they recognise the legitimacy of the legal system within which the judge operates.

The authority of a military officer over his troops is recognised because the officer holds a commission granted by the state, has been elevated through an established chain of command, and exercises authority defined by military regulations. Soldiers obey because they recognise the legitimacy of the military hierarchy, not because the officer personally compels them.

The authority of a democratically elected prime minister is recognised because the prime minister came to office through free elections, governs within constitutional constraints, and exercises powers defined by law. Citizens comply because they accept the legitimacy of the democratic process that produced the government.
definition: An agent A has authority over a group G if and only if (i) A occupies an office or role recognised within a legitimate institutional framework, (ii) A's directives fall within the scope of that office, and (iii) A came to occupy the office through procedures recognised as valid by the framework.
conditions: A occupies an office within a legitimate institutional framework
A's directives fall within the scope of that office
A came to the office through recognised procedures
counterexample: Consider a village elder in a small, pre-institutional community. There is no written constitution, no formal office of 'elder,' and no codified procedure by which one becomes an elder. The elder's authority arises from long experience, demonstrated wisdom, and the gradual accumulation of communal trust over decades. The villagers defer to the elder's judgement on matters of dispute, resource allocation, and communal decision. Yet the elder occupies no formally defined office, the scope of the elder's guidance is not institutionally delimited, and no codified procedure elevated the elder to this role. None of the three conditions is met. Yet the elder exercises genuine authority — authority that the villagers recognise and respect.
abductive_insight: The background cases — judge, military officer, prime minister — all operate within formal institutional frameworks: legal systems, military hierarchies, constitutional democracies. The definition captures the institutional character of authority in these settings but cannot accommodate authority that arises informally, through the gradual accrual of trust and reputation, in communities that lack formal institutions. The background cases represent authority in its institutionalised form, and the definition mistakes the features of institutional authority for necessary features of authority as such.
passage:
The cases in which authority is most clearly exercised and most readily analysed share a common institutional structure. The judge exercises authority within a legal system that defines the office, specifies its powers, and prescribes the procedures of appointment. The military officer operates within a hierarchy established by the state, with powers defined by regulation and appointment determined by commission. The prime minister governs within a constitutional framework, empowered by election and constrained by law. In each case, authority is institutional: it is conferred by a framework, exercised within defined limits, and acquired through recognised procedures.

One might codify these cases by defining authority as the occupation of an office within a legitimate institutional framework, the issuance of directives within the scope of that office, and accession through recognised procedures. This definition captures the institutional authority of the paradigm cases with precision. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is provided by pre-institutional communities. Consider a village elder in a small, traditional community that possesses no written laws, no formal offices, and no codified procedures. The elder has lived in the village for decades. Through long experience, demonstrated good judgement, and the gradual accumulation of the community's trust, the elder has come to be the person to whom the villagers turn in matters of dispute, allocation of resources, and collective decision. They defer to the elder's judgement not because a constitution says they must but because they have learned, over many years, that the elder's counsel is wise. The elder occupies no formally defined office. The scope of the elder's guidance is not delimited by any institutional document. No codified procedure elevated the elder to this position. None of the three conditions of the definition is met. Yet the elder exercises genuine authority — authority that is recognised, respected, and willingly deferred to. The definition, built from cases of institutional authority, cannot accommodate this informal, emergent, and deeply human form of authority that predates institutions and may be more fundamental than they.

background_cases: The refusal of certain Quakers in colonial Pennsylvania to pay taxes earmarked for military purposes was regarded as a legitimate act of civil disobedience because the Quakers acted openly, accepted the legal penalties for their refusal, and grounded their disobedience in a moral principle — the prohibition of participation in violence — that they were prepared to defend publicly.

The refusal of Henry David Thoreau to pay his poll tax in protest against the Mexican-American War and slavery was regarded as an act of civil disobedience because Thoreau acted openly, accepted imprisonment, and justified his refusal by appeal to a moral principle that he articulated at length in his celebrated essay.

The campaign of passive resistance led by Mohandas Gandhi against the British salt tax in India was regarded as civil disobedience because the participants acted openly, accepted arrest and imprisonment, and grounded their defiance in the moral principle that an unjust law has no claim to obedience.
definition: An act A by a citizen C constitutes civil disobedience if and only if (i) A is a deliberate violation of a law that C regards as unjust, (ii) A is performed openly and publicly, and (iii) C accepts the legal consequences of A — that is, C does not attempt to evade punishment.
conditions: A is a deliberate violation of a law regarded as unjust
A is performed openly and publicly
C accepts the legal consequences
counterexample: Consider a group of citizens who, in protest against a law requiring the registration of members of a persecuted minority, deliberately violate the registration law by sheltering unregistered members of the minority in their homes. Their violation is deliberate, and they regard the law as unjust. But they do not act openly: they conceal the presence of the sheltered persons, because publicity would lead to the arrest and deportation of those they are protecting. They do not accept the legal consequences: they take care to avoid detection, because their arrest would leave the sheltered persons without protection. Conditions (ii) and (iii) fail. Yet their action has all the moral seriousness and political character of civil disobedience — it is motivated by conscience, directed against an unjust law, and undertaken at considerable personal risk.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the Quakers, Thoreau, Gandhi — all involve disobedience against laws whose enforcement does not endanger third parties. The Quakers could refuse to pay military taxes openly because the consequences of their openness fell only upon themselves. Thoreau could accept imprisonment because no one else was harmed by his imprisonment. Gandhi's followers could accept arrest because the salt tax affected everyone equally. The definition's requirements of openness and acceptance of consequences are appropriate when the disobedient citizen risks only her own welfare, but they become morally inappropriate when compliance with these requirements would endanger the very persons the disobedience is meant to protect.
passage:
The acts most commonly cited as paradigms of civil disobedience share a cluster of features that have been codified into a standard definition. The Quakers of colonial Pennsylvania refused to pay military taxes openly, submitted to the penalties, and justified their refusal by appeal to a moral principle they were prepared to defend in public. Thoreau refused to pay his poll tax, went to gaol, and wrote a celebrated essay explaining his reasons. Gandhi and his followers broke the salt law publicly, accepted mass arrest, and articulated the moral grounds of their defiance for all to hear. In each case, the disobedient citizen acted in the open, accepted the consequences, and offered a moral justification.

One might codify these cases by defining civil disobedience as a deliberate, open violation of a law regarded as unjust, accompanied by acceptance of the legal consequences. This captures the paradigm cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when the law being protested endangers persons other than the disobedient citizen. Consider a group of citizens who, in conscience, refuse to comply with a law requiring the registration of members of a persecuted minority — a law whose enforcement would lead to the arrest and deportation of those registered. The citizens shelter unregistered members of the minority in their homes. Their violation of the law is deliberate, and they regard the law as profoundly unjust. But they cannot act openly, because publicity would reveal the presence of the persons they are sheltering and subject them to the very persecution the disobedience is meant to prevent. They cannot accept the legal consequences, because their arrest would leave the sheltered persons without protection. The definition's conditions of openness and acceptance are violated. Yet their action possesses every moral feature of civil disobedience: it is conscientious, principled, directed against an unjust law, and undertaken at grave personal risk. The background cases all involved disobedience whose consequences fell upon the disobedient citizen alone. The definition's requirements of openness and acceptance of punishment are appropriate in such cases but become morally untenable when the disobedience is undertaken to protect others whose safety depends upon secrecy.

background_cases: The government of a constitutional republic that holds regular elections, respects civil liberties, and governs according to law is regarded as legitimate because its authority derives from the consent of the governed, expressed through the electoral process, and its exercise of power is constrained by constitutional provisions that the governed have endorsed.

A constitutional monarchy in which the monarch reigns but does not govern, and in which effective power is exercised by a parliament elected by universal suffrage, is regarded as legitimate because the parliament's authority rests on popular consent and its power is exercised within constitutional limits accepted by the population.

A federal democracy in which power is divided between a central government and regional governments, each elected by the citizens within its jurisdiction, is regarded as legitimate because each level of government derives its authority from the consent of the citizens it governs and operates within a constitutional framework that distributes power according to principles the citizens endorse.
definition: A government G is politically legitimate if and only if (i) G derives its authority from the consent of the governed, (ii) G exercises its power within the limits set by a constitution or fundamental law, and (iii) the governed have the ability to remove G from power through peaceful, institutionalised procedures.
conditions: G derives authority from consent of the governed
G exercises power within constitutional limits
The governed can remove G through peaceful procedures
counterexample: Consider a revolutionary government that has just seized power in the wake of a popular uprising against a corrupt and oppressive regime. The revolution was supported by the overwhelming majority of the population. The new government has not yet held elections — there has been no time — and it has not yet adopted a constitution. It governs by decree, guided by the principles of the revolution. The governed cannot yet remove it through peaceful procedures, because no such procedures have been established. Conditions (i), (ii), and (iii) all fail in the formal sense: there has been no electoral consent, no constitutional framework, and no institutionalised mechanism for removal. Yet the government commands the genuine support of the population, it governs in accordance with the principles for which the people rose, and it is widely regarded — both domestically and internationally — as more legitimate than the regime it replaced.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the constitutional republic, the constitutional monarchy, the federal democracy — are all established political orders in which consent, constitutional constraint, and peaceful removal have been institutionalised over time. The definition captures the features of mature, stable democratic orders but cannot accommodate the transitional legitimacy of revolutionary governments that enjoy genuine popular support but have not yet had the opportunity to institutionalise the mechanisms the definition requires. What the background cases share at a deeper level is not the formal mechanisms of consent and constitutional governance but the genuine correspondence between the government's actions and the will of the governed — a correspondence that can exist before formal institutions are in place.
passage:
The governments most confidently regarded as legitimate share a common institutional structure. The constitutional republic derives its authority from elections, exercises power within constitutional limits, and provides for the peaceful transfer of power. The constitutional monarchy achieves the same ends through a different arrangement of institutions. The federal democracy distributes these features across multiple levels of government. In each case, legitimacy rests upon a set of institutions — elections, constitutions, procedures for removal — that formalise the relationship between the government and the governed.

One might codify this by defining legitimacy as the condition in which a government derives its authority from consent, exercises power within constitutional limits, and can be removed by peaceful procedures. This definition captures the institutional foundations of the paradigm cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the revolutionary government that takes power with the overwhelming support of the population after the overthrow of a corrupt and oppressive regime. The revolution was genuinely popular — the old regime had lost all support, and the new government embodies the aspirations for which the people rose. But the new government has not yet held elections; there has been no formal expression of consent. It has not yet adopted a constitution; it governs by decree, guided by revolutionary principles. It has not yet established procedures for its own removal. All three conditions of the definition fail. Yet the government is widely regarded as legitimate — more legitimate, indeed, than the constitutional regime it replaced, which satisfied the definition's conditions while governing corruptly and oppressively. The background cases are all mature political orders in which the mechanisms of consent and constitutional constraint have been built up over generations. The definition captures the features of these stable orders but cannot accommodate the transitional legitimacy of a government that enjoys the genuine support of the governed but has not yet had the opportunity to formalise that support in the institutions the definition requires.

background_cases: A member of parliament who was elected by the voters of a specific constituency and who regularly consults those voters, holds public meetings, and votes in parliament according to their expressed wishes is regarded as a representative because her actions in parliament reflect the preferences of those who chose her.

A trade union delegate who is elected by the members of her local branch and who carries their resolutions to the national conference, speaking and voting as they have instructed, is regarded as a representative because she acts as a conduit for the expressed will of those who elected her.

A diplomat who is appointed by his government and who negotiates on behalf of his country according to the instructions he has received is regarded as a representative because his actions in the negotiation reflect the positions of the government that appointed him.
definition: A person R is a political representative of a group G if and only if (i) R has been authorised by G to act on G's behalf, (ii) R acts in accordance with the expressed preferences of G, and (iii) R is accountable to G — that is, G has the power to sanction R for failing to act in accordance with G's preferences.
conditions: R is authorised by G to act on G's behalf
R acts in accordance with G's expressed preferences
R is accountable to G
counterexample: Consider a member of parliament who was elected by her constituency but who, on a matter of grave moral importance — the abolition of capital punishment, let us say — votes against the clearly expressed wishes of her constituents because she believes, after careful reflection, that capital punishment is morally wrong. She is authorised by her constituents, and she is accountable to them — they may vote her out at the next election. But she does not act in accordance with their expressed preferences; she acts according to her own moral judgement. Condition (ii) is violated. Yet a long and distinguished tradition in political thought — associated with Edmund Burke and others — holds that she is acting precisely as a representative should: exercising her own judgement on behalf of her constituents rather than merely transmitting their preferences. On this view, her independence of judgement is not a failure of representation but its highest form.
abductive_insight: The background cases — the constituency MP, the union delegate, the diplomat — all involve representatives acting in relatively routine matters where the preferences of the represented group are clear and uncontested. In such cases, acting in accordance with expressed preferences is unproblematic. But the definition assumes that representation always consists in the faithful transmission of preferences, and it cannot accommodate the view — held by many thoughtful political theorists — that a representative may sometimes best serve her constituents by exercising independent judgement, particularly on matters where the constituents' expressed preferences may be ill-informed or morally questionable.
passage:
The persons most straightforwardly classified as political representatives share a common relationship to those they represent. A constituency member of parliament consults her voters and votes as they wish. A union delegate carries the resolutions of her branch to the national conference. A diplomat negotiates according to his government's instructions. In each case, the representative acts as a faithful transmitter of the preferences of those who authorised her to act.

One might codify this relationship by defining representation as the condition in which a person authorised by a group acts in accordance with the group's preferences and is accountable to the group for doing so. This definition captures the paradigm cases admirably. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is the conscientious parliamentarian who votes against her constituents' clearly expressed wishes on a matter of deep moral significance. Consider a member of parliament whose constituents strongly favour the retention of capital punishment. After careful study and reflection, she concludes that capital punishment is morally indefensible and votes for its abolition. She has been authorised by her constituents, and she is accountable to them — they may remove her at the next election. But she has not acted in accordance with their expressed preferences. Condition (ii) is violated. The definition classifies her as having failed in her representative role. Yet a substantial tradition in political thought holds that she has acted as a representative of the highest kind — one who exercises her own judgement on behalf of her constituents rather than merely echoing their wishes. On this view, the representative's task is not to transmit preferences but to deliberate wisely, and her independence of judgement is not a defect but the very essence of representation. The background cases all involved routine matters where preferences were clear and uncontested. The definition captures representation as the transmission of preferences but cannot accommodate representation as the exercise of judgement — a conception that becomes indispensable precisely when the represented group's preferences are most in need of thoughtful scrutiny.

background_cases: In a well-functioning constitutional monarchy, the monarch exercises authority not merely through the capacity to compel but through a tradition of governance that the population regards as legitimate; the subjects comply with royal edicts in part because they recognise the monarch's right to govern, a right grounded in custom, tradition, and constitutional convention.

In a well-functioning republic, elected officials exercise authority because the population regards them as having been chosen through fair and open procedures; citizens comply with legislation not merely out of fear of punishment but because they recognise that the officials were selected by a process they endorse.

In a well-functioning religious community, the elders exercise authority because the members of the community regard the elders as possessing wisdom and spiritual standing that entitle them to govern communal affairs; compliance is grounded in respect for the elders' qualities, not in fear of coercion.
definition: A person or institution A has political authority over a group G if and only if (i) A issues directives that purport to bind the members of G, (ii) A possesses the power to enforce these directives through coercion, and (iii) the members of G generally comply with A's directives.
conditions: A issues directives that purport to bind the members of G
A possesses the power to enforce these directives through coercion
The members of G generally comply with A's directives
counterexample: Consider a bandit chief who controls a mountain pass and issues directives to all travellers — demanding tolls, prescribing routes, forbidding certain goods. The bandit possesses the coercive power to enforce these directives, and travellers generally comply. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the bandit does not possess political authority. Travellers comply because they fear violence, not because they recognise any right on the bandit's part to issue directives.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve a normative dimension absent from the bandit case: in each, the population's compliance is grounded not merely in the threat of coercion but in a recognition of the authority's legitimacy — whether that legitimacy derives from tradition, democratic selection, or spiritual standing. The definition captures the coercive and behavioural dimensions of authority but omits the requirement of legitimacy, which is what distinguishes political authority from the mere exercise of organised coercive power.
passage:
When one examines the institutions most clearly recognised as possessing political authority — a constitutional monarchy, a republic, a religious community with established governance — a common feature emerges. In each case, the subjects or citizens comply with directives not merely because they fear coercion but because they recognise the authority's right to govern. The monarch's authority rests on tradition and constitutional convention. The elected official's authority rests on the fairness of the selection process. The elder's authority rests on the community's recognition of wisdom and spiritual standing. In each case, compliance is grounded in a perception of legitimacy.

One might attempt to define political authority as the condition in which a person or institution issues binding directives, possesses the coercive power to enforce them, and secures general compliance. This definition captures the behavioural and coercive dimensions of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency concerns a bandit chief who controls a mountain pass. The bandit issues directives to travellers — demanding tolls, prescribing routes, forbidding certain goods. He possesses the power to enforce these directives through violence, and travellers generally comply. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that the bandit does not possess political authority. His power is merely coercive. What the background cases possess, and what the bandit lacks, is the recognition of legitimacy on the part of those who comply. The definition captures the external features of authority but omits the normative foundation — the sense in which authority involves not merely the power to compel but the right to govern.

background_cases: In the Athenian assembly, citizens debated proposed laws before voting upon them; the process of debate was regarded as essential to the democratic character of the assembly, for it was through public argument and deliberation that citizens arrived at informed and considered judgements about the common good.

In the parliamentary tradition, proposed legislation is subjected to extended debate in which members of parliament present arguments, raise objections, and propose amendments; the deliberative process is regarded as essential to the democratic legitimacy of the resulting legislation, for it ensures that laws are enacted only after sustained examination of their merits.

In the New England town meeting, all citizens gathered to discuss and vote upon local matters; the meeting was not merely an occasion for voting but a forum for deliberation, in which citizens listened to one another's arguments and modified their views in light of the discussion before casting their votes.
definition: A political system is a democracy if and only if (i) political decisions are made by majority vote of the eligible population, (ii) all adult members of the community are eligible to vote, and (iii) votes are cast by secret ballot.
conditions: Political decisions are made by majority vote of the eligible population
All adult members of the community are eligible to vote
Votes are cast by secret ballot
counterexample: Consider a political system in which all adults vote by secret ballot on every political decision, but in which there is no public debate, no forum for the exchange of arguments, and no opportunity for citizens to deliberate about the merits of proposed policies before voting. Citizens vote on the basis of whatever information and prejudices they happen to possess, without any structured opportunity to hear opposing views or to subject their own views to critical scrutiny. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that such a system, whilst formally democratic, lacks the deliberative dimension that gives democratic procedures their capacity to track the common good.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve a deliberative process — debate in the Athenian assembly, parliamentary discussion, town meeting deliberation — that precedes and informs the act of voting. It is this deliberative process that gives democratic decision-making its distinctive character, distinguishing it from mere aggregation of uninformed preferences. The definition captures the aggregative dimension of democracy — majority rule, universal suffrage, secret ballot — but omits the requirement of deliberation, which is what enables citizens to arrive at informed and considered judgements.
passage:
When one examines the political institutions most securely regarded as democratic — the Athenian assembly, the parliamentary tradition, the New England town meeting — a feature emerges that goes beyond the mechanics of voting. In each case, the act of voting is preceded by a process of deliberation. In the Athenian assembly, citizens debated proposed laws before casting their votes. In parliament, legislation is subjected to extended debate, with arguments, objections, and amendments. In the town meeting, citizens listened to one another's views and modified their positions before the vote. It is this deliberative process that gives democratic decision-making its distinctive character — its capacity to produce decisions that are informed, considered, and responsive to the common good.

One might define democracy as a system in which political decisions are made by majority vote of all eligible adults casting secret ballots. This definition captures the aggregative dimension of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency is a political system in which all adults vote by secret ballot on every question, but in which there is no public debate, no forum for argument, and no opportunity for deliberation before voting. Citizens vote on the basis of whatever information and prejudices they happen to possess, with no structured opportunity to hear opposing views. All three conditions are met. Yet one might observe that such a system, whilst formally democratic, lacks the deliberative dimension that the background cases possess. What makes the Athenian assembly, the parliament, and the town meeting genuinely democratic is not merely that they aggregate votes but that they provide a forum in which citizens can deliberate — and it is this requirement that the definition has omitted.

background_cases: The right to freedom of speech is characteristically understood as a right that holds against the state: the state is obliged not to silence the citizen, and this obligation is not contingent upon the citizen's good behaviour or social utility but attaches to the citizen simply in virtue of being a person; the right is held to be inalienable — it cannot be forfeited, waived, or transferred.

The right to freedom of worship is characteristically understood as a right that the citizen holds regardless of the content of his religious beliefs; the state is obliged to permit worship even when the majority disapproves of the religion in question, and this obligation is not contingent upon the social utility of the religious practice; the right attaches to the person as such.

The right not to be subjected to torture is characteristically understood as absolute: the state is obliged to refrain from torturing any person, regardless of the circumstances, and this obligation cannot be overridden by considerations of public safety or national interest; the right is held to be inherent in the person and not dependent upon legal recognition for its moral force.
definition: A person P has a right to X if and only if (i) there is a corresponding obligation on the part of others — whether individuals or the state — not to interfere with P's access to X, (ii) P is entitled to claim X, and (iii) P's access to X serves the common good.
conditions: There is a corresponding obligation on the part of others not to interfere with P's access to X
P is entitled to claim X
P's access to X serves the common good
counterexample: Consider a political dissident in an authoritarian state who exercises her right to freedom of speech by publishing criticisms of the government. Her speech does not serve the common good as the government understands it — indeed, it destabilises public order and undermines the ruling party's authority. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that the dissident's right to speak is precisely the kind of right that is most important to protect, and that a definition which makes rights contingent upon their serving the common good would strip rights of their protective function in precisely the cases where that function is most needed.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involve rights that are understood as attaching to the person as such — not contingent upon their social utility or their contribution to the common good. The right to speak, the right to worship, and the right not to be tortured are held to be inalienable precisely because they do not depend upon a calculus of public benefit. The definition captures the obligational and entitlement dimensions of rights but introduces a condition — service to the common good — that is foreign to the very concept of a right as it figures in the background cases. What those cases reveal is that rights characteristically function as protections against the subordination of the individual to collective interests, and a definition that makes rights contingent upon collective benefit has undermined this protective function.
passage:
When one examines the rights most firmly established in political thought — freedom of speech, freedom of worship, the prohibition of torture — a common feature emerges. Each is understood as attaching to the individual as such, not as contingent upon its contribution to the common good. The right to speak freely is held even when speech is unpopular or destabilising. The right to worship is maintained even when the majority disapproves of the religion in question. The right not to be tortured is absolute, regardless of what public benefit might be achieved by extracting information through coercion. These rights function as protections of the individual against the demands of the collective.

One might define a right as a condition in which there is a corresponding obligation on others not to interfere, the person is entitled to make a claim, and the person's access to the good in question serves the common interest. This definition captures the obligational and entitlement dimensions of the paradigmatic cases. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency concerns a political dissident who publishes criticisms of an authoritarian government. Her speech does not serve the common good as understood by the state — it destabilises public order and undermines the ruling authority. The third condition fails. Yet one might observe that the dissident's right to speak is the very paradigm of a right worth protecting. What the background cases reveal is that rights characteristically protect the individual against the subordination of her interests to collective benefit. A definition that makes rights contingent upon serving the common good has undermined the protective function that is essential to the concept.

background_cases: In the Athenian assembly, citizens debated proposed laws in open forum, heard arguments for and against, questioned the proposers, and voted only after extended deliberation; the democratic character of the process lay not merely in the act of voting but in the quality of the public reasoning that preceded it.

In the New England town meeting, citizens gathered to discuss local matters — road repairs, school budgets, land-use regulations — hearing from their neighbours, raising objections, and reaching decisions through a process of collective deliberation that gave every participant an opportunity to shape the outcome.

In the great parliamentary debates over the abolition of the slave trade, members spoke at length, marshalled arguments, responded to objections, and changed their votes in light of what they heard; the democratic legitimacy of the outcome rested on the quality and sincerity of the deliberative process, not merely on the final tally.
definition: A political system G is a democracy if and only if (i) the ultimate political authority resides in the votes of the citizens, (ii) elections are held at regular intervals, and (iii) the results of elections are binding on the government.
conditions: The ultimate political authority resides in the votes of the citizens
Elections are held at regular intervals
The results of elections are binding on the government
counterexample: Consider a state in which citizens vote at regular intervals and the results are binding. Yet the only information available to voters is provided by the government itself: there is no independent press, no freedom of public debate, and no opportunity for citizens to hear arguments against the government's preferred policies. Citizens cast their ballots, and the ballots are honestly counted. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet one might observe that the citizens are voting in an informational vacuum, and the elections, though formally free, are substantively hollow.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the mere mechanics of voting cannot capture: in each, the democratic process included a substantive deliberative component — public reasoning, open debate, the hearing of opposing arguments — that enabled citizens to form considered judgements before casting their votes. The information-starved electorate satisfies the procedural conditions of democracy but lacks the deliberative dimension that gave the Athenian assembly, the New England town meeting, and the parliamentary debate their democratic character. The definition captures the aggregative mechanism of democracy but omits the deliberative process that gives that mechanism its legitimacy.
passage:
When one examines the historical instances most often celebrated as exemplary democracies, a feature emerges that goes beyond the mechanics of voting. In the Athenian assembly, citizens did not merely cast ballots; they debated, questioned, and deliberated in open forum before reaching a decision. In the New England town meeting, citizens gathered to hear arguments, raise objections, and reason together about local affairs. In the great parliamentary debates over the slave trade, members marshalled evidence, responded to objections, and occasionally changed their votes in light of what they heard. In each case, the democratic character of the process lay not merely in the act of voting but in the quality of the public deliberation that preceded it.

One might attempt to define democracy by requiring that ultimate political authority reside in the citizens' votes, that elections be held at regular intervals, and that election results be binding on the government. This definition captures the procedural core of democratic governance. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one imagines a state in which citizens vote regularly and the results are honestly counted and binding, but in which the only information available to voters is provided by the government. There is no independent press, no freedom of public discussion, and no opportunity for citizens to hear arguments against the government's preferred policies. The citizens vote, and their votes are faithfully tallied. All three conditions are met. Yet the elections are substantively hollow: citizens cannot form considered judgements because they lack access to the information and the competing arguments that would enable them to do so. What the background cases possess and this case lacks is a deliberative dimension — the opportunity for public reasoning, open debate, and the hearing of opposing views. The definition, by attending only to the aggregative mechanism of voting, cannot distinguish elections that are the outcome of genuine deliberation from elections conducted in an informational void.

background_cases: When the abolition of serfdom in nineteenth-century Russia freed the peasants from legal bondage to their landlords, the liberation was significant precisely because it was accompanied by the allocation of land — however inadequate — that gave the freed serfs a material basis upon which to exercise their newly won liberty; freedom without land would have been freedom in name only.

When the passage of civil rights legislation in the mid-twentieth century removed legal barriers to public accommodation and voting, the significance of the legislation lay in the fact that it was accompanied by enforcement mechanisms — federal registrars, court orders, the deployment of marshals — that gave the new legal rights practical effect; the rights without enforcement would have been empty pronouncements.

When the establishment of universal public education gave working-class families access to schooling, the significance of the reform lay in the fact that it provided the material conditions — buildings, teachers, books — that enabled families to exercise the right to education; a legal right to education without schools would have been meaningless.
definition: A citizen C is free with respect to an action A if and only if (i) no law prohibits C from performing A, (ii) no person or institution physically prevents C from performing A, and (iii) C would not be punished for performing A.
conditions: No law prohibits C from performing A
No person or institution physically prevents C from performing A
C would not be punished for performing A
counterexample: Consider a citizen in a society where no law prohibits her from starting a business, no person physically prevents her, and she would not be punished for doing so. Yet she is illiterate, has no access to capital, and lives in a remote area with no infrastructure. She is free to start a business in the sense that nothing prohibits, prevents, or punishes, but she utterly lacks the capacity to do so. All three conditions are met, yet calling her free to start a business seems to describe a merely formal possibility that has no bearing on her actual situation.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the citizen's case: in each, the removal of a legal or social barrier was accompanied by the provision of material conditions that made the newly won freedom exercisable. The freed serfs received land, the enfranchised received enforcement, the working-class families received schools. What the definition captures is the absence of prohibition and prevention, but what the background cases reveal is that genuine freedom requires not merely the removal of obstacles but the presence of enabling conditions. The definition, built entirely on the absence of interference, cannot distinguish between a freedom one can exercise and a freedom one merely possesses on paper.
passage:
When one examines the historical moments at which freedom has been most significantly expanded, a common feature emerges that goes beyond the mere removal of prohibition and prevention. The abolition of serfdom in Russia was significant because it was accompanied — however imperfectly — by the allocation of land that gave the freed peasants a material basis for their liberty. The civil rights legislation of the twentieth century was significant because it was backed by enforcement mechanisms that translated legal rights into practical realities. The establishment of universal public education was significant because it provided the buildings, teachers, and books that made the right to education exercisable.

One might attempt to characterise freedom by requiring that no law prohibit the action, no person or institution physically prevent it, and no punishment attach to it. This purely negative analysis captures an important dimension of freedom. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers a citizen in a society that imposes no legal prohibition on starting a business, physically prevents no one from doing so, and attaches no punishment to the attempt. The citizen, however, is illiterate, possesses no capital, and lives in a remote area without roads, markets, or communication infrastructure. She is free to start a business in the sense that nothing prohibits, prevents, or punishes. Yet she utterly lacks the capacity to exercise this freedom. All three conditions of the definition are met, yet her freedom is purely nominal. What the background cases possess and her situation lacks is the presence of enabling conditions — land, enforcement, schools — that make formal freedom exercisable. It appears that the definition, by confining freedom to the absence of interference, cannot distinguish genuine, effective freedom from the mere absence of prohibition.

background_cases: When a constitutional court strikes down a statute as unconstitutional, the legislature typically complies not because the court commands superior force — it commands no force at all — but because the court's authority is grounded in a shared recognition of the constitutional order; the legislature complies because it accepts that the court has the right to adjudicate such matters.

When a democratically elected parliament enacts a tax law, citizens generally comply not merely because of the threat of penalties but because they recognise the parliament's authority as grounded in democratic processes — elections, representation, deliberation — that confer a right to legislate; the law carries normative weight beyond its coercive backing.

When the elected leader of a trade union calls a strike, the members follow not because the leader can compel them — she cannot — but because they recognise her authority as grounded in the democratic processes of the union: she was elected, she consulted the membership, and the decision was reached through established procedures; the authority rests on legitimacy, not on power.
definition: A person or institution A has political authority over a group G if and only if (i) A issues directives to the members of G, (ii) A has the power to enforce compliance with those directives, and (iii) A is recognised by an external body — such as another state or an international organisation — as the rightful authority over G.
conditions: A issues directives to the members of G
A has the power to enforce compliance with those directives
A is recognised by an external body as the rightful authority over G
counterexample: Consider an occupying military force that issues directives to the population of a conquered territory, possesses overwhelming power to enforce compliance, and is recognised by several allied states and by an international organisation as the de facto authority over the territory. All three conditions are met. Yet the population does not accept the authority of the occupying force, and the directives carry no normative weight in the eyes of those to whom they are addressed. The occupying force rules by power, not by authority.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature that the occupying force conspicuously lacks: in each, the authority is grounded in an internal relationship between the authority and those subject to it — the legislature accepts the court's constitutional role, citizens accept the parliament's democratic mandate, union members accept their leader's elected status. The authority rests on a shared recognition of legitimacy by those who are governed. External recognition by other states or organisations is neither necessary nor sufficient for this internal legitimacy. The definition, by locating the ground of authority in external recognition and enforcement power, mistakes the international dimension of sovereignty for the domestic dimension of legitimate rule.
passage:
When one examines the cases in which political authority is most securely exercised, a common feature emerges that cannot be reduced to the power to enforce or to recognition by external bodies. When a constitutional court strikes down a statute, the legislature complies not because the court can compel it but because both institutions share a recognition of the constitutional framework that gives the court the right to adjudicate. When a parliament levies taxes, citizens pay not merely to avoid penalties but because they accept, however grudgingly, the parliament's democratic mandate. When a union leader calls a strike, the members follow because they recognise her authority as grounded in the union's democratic procedures.

One might attempt to define political authority by requiring that a person or institution issue directives, possess the power to enforce them, and be recognised by an external body as the rightful authority. This definition captures the coercive and international dimensions of political rule. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises with military occupation. Consider an occupying force that issues directives to the population of a conquered territory, possesses overwhelming military power to enforce compliance, and is recognised by several allied states and by an international organisation as the de facto authority. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the population does not accept the occupying force's authority. The directives carry no normative weight in their eyes — they comply from fear, not from recognition of a right to rule. What the background cases possess and the occupation lacks is an internal relationship of legitimacy between the authority and the governed. The court's authority rests on the legislature's acceptance of the constitutional order; the parliament's authority rests on the citizens' acceptance of the democratic process; the union leader's authority rests on the members' acceptance of the election. It appears that the definition, by grounding authority in external recognition and enforcement power, cannot capture the internal dimension of legitimacy that distinguishes genuine authority from mere domination.

background_cases: In the Athenian democracy of the fifth century BCE, citizens participated directly in legislative deliberation, debating and voting on laws in the assembly; the system's democratic character derived not merely from the existence of voting but from the active participation of citizens in the process of reasoning about public affairs.

In the New England town meeting tradition, citizens gathered to discuss and decide local matters by direct deliberation; the democratic quality of these assemblies lay not merely in the act of voting but in the public exchange of reasons and arguments that preceded the vote.

In certain Swiss cantons, the Landsgemeinde — an open-air assembly of citizens — combined deliberation with direct voting on cantonal legislation; the democratic character of the institution was inseparable from the deliberative process in which citizens were expected to listen, argue, and persuade before casting their votes.
definition: A political system G is democratic if and only if (i) all adult citizens of G have the right to vote, (ii) elections are held at regular intervals, and (iii) the government of G is determined by the outcome of these elections.
conditions: All adult citizens have the right to vote
Elections are held at regular intervals
The government is determined by the outcome of elections
counterexample: Consider a state in which all adult citizens have the right to vote, elections are held at regular intervals, and the government is determined by the electoral outcome. All three conditions are met. Yet between elections, citizens have no means of participating in governance. There are no public forums, no mechanisms for petition or initiative, no consultative processes, and no channels through which citizens can communicate their views to their representatives. The citizens vote every four years and are thereafter entirely passive. The government governs without any input from the citizenry between elections. One might observe that such a system, though it meets every condition of the definition, is democratic only in the thinnest possible sense.
abductive_insight: The background cases reveal that the democratic quality of political systems depends not merely on voting but on the active participation of citizens in deliberation about public affairs. The Athenian assembly, the New England town meeting, and the Swiss Landsgemeinde are democratic not because citizens vote but because citizens reason, argue, and deliberate together before deciding. The state that votes every four years and is otherwise passive satisfies every condition of the definition yet lacks the deliberative dimension that, in the paradigmatic cases, constitutes the very substance of democratic life. The definition captures the electoral mechanism but omits the participatory and deliberative dimensions that give democracy its distinctive character.
passage:
When one examines the political systems that have been most widely celebrated as democratic, a feature emerges that goes beyond the mechanics of voting. The Athenian assembly was democratic not merely because citizens voted but because they gathered to debate, argue, and reason together about the laws under which they would live. The New England town meeting was democratic not merely because it produced decisions by majority vote but because those decisions were preceded by an open exchange of reasons and arguments among the citizens assembled. The Swiss Landsgemeinde combined deliberation with direct voting, and its democratic character was inseparable from the process of public reasoning that preceded each ballot.

One might attempt to characterise democracy as a political system in which all adult citizens have the right to vote, elections are held at regular intervals, and the government is determined by electoral outcomes. This procedural definition captures the electoral machinery of modern representative government. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers a state that reduces democratic participation to the act of voting alone. Suppose all adult citizens have the right to vote, elections are held regularly, and the government is duly determined by their outcome. Yet between elections there are no public forums, no consultative mechanisms, no opportunities for petition or initiative, and no channels of communication between citizens and their representatives. Citizens vote every four years and are otherwise entirely passive. The government governs in complete isolation from the citizenry between elections. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet one might observe that such a system is democratic in form alone. What the background cases possess and the passive-voting state lacks is the participatory and deliberative dimension — the active engagement of citizens in reasoning about public affairs. It appears that the definition, by reducing democracy to voting and elections, cannot distinguish a vibrant democratic polity from a system in which citizens are periodically summoned to choose rulers and are otherwise excluded from political life.

background_cases: When a court awards compensation to a worker injured by an employer's negligence, the award is just not merely because it follows established legal procedure but because it rectifies a specific wrong — the employer's failure to maintain safe conditions — and restores, so far as money can, the balance that the wrong disturbed.

When a state redistributes resources through progressive taxation to fund public education, the redistribution is justified not merely by the political authority of the state but by the recognition that children born into poverty lack opportunities available to those born into wealth, and that justice requires some correction of this inequality.

When an international tribunal orders reparations for a state that violated the territory of its neighbour, the order is just not merely because it follows the rules of international law but because it addresses a specific injury — the violation of sovereignty — and seeks to restore the status quo ante.
definition: A distribution of goods is just if and only if (i) it results from the consistent application of a rule to all members of the relevant group, (ii) the rule is applied impartially — that is, without favouritism towards any particular individual, and (iii) the rule was established by a legitimate authority.
conditions: The distribution results from the consistent application of a rule
The rule is applied impartially
The rule was established by a legitimate authority
counterexample: Consider a state that establishes a rule — enacted by a legitimate authority, applied consistently and impartially — distributing medical care on the basis of a lottery. Every citizen, regardless of need, has an equal chance of receiving treatment. The rule is applied without favouritism. It was enacted by a democratically elected legislature. All three conditions are met. Yet under this system, a person with a life-threatening illness has no greater claim to medical care than a person with a trivial complaint. The distribution is procedurally impeccable but substantively unjust, for it ignores the morally relevant differences between cases.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the medical lottery: in each, the distribution is responsive to morally relevant features of the particular case — the severity of the injury, the depth of the disadvantage, the gravity of the violation. The court considers the specific harm suffered by the worker. The tax system considers the specific inequality of opportunity. The international tribunal considers the specific injury to sovereignty. The medical lottery, by contrast, is entirely indifferent to the morally relevant differences between those who receive and those who do not receive treatment. The definition captures procedural fairness — consistency, impartiality, legitimate authority — but omits the substantive dimension of justice: responsiveness to the morally relevant features of particular cases.
passage:
When one examines the distributions and rectifications that we most confidently call just, a common feature emerges that goes beyond procedural regularity. The court that compensates an injured worker does not merely apply a rule consistently; it attends to the specific harm the worker suffered and the specific wrong that caused it. The state that funds public education through progressive taxation does not merely apply a rule impartially; it recognises the specific inequality of opportunity that disadvantages children born into poverty. The international tribunal that orders reparations does not merely follow established procedures; it addresses the specific injury inflicted by one state upon another. In each case, justice is achieved not by the mechanical application of a rule but by responsiveness to the morally relevant features of the particular situation.

One might attempt to characterise a just distribution as one resulting from the consistent and impartial application of a rule established by a legitimate authority. This procedural definition captures the formal requirements of fairness. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers a distribution that is procedurally impeccable but substantively indifferent to morally relevant differences. Suppose a state establishes, through proper democratic procedures, a rule distributing medical care by lottery. Every citizen has an equal chance of receiving treatment, regardless of the nature or severity of the condition. The rule is applied consistently and without favouritism. It was enacted by a legitimate authority. All three conditions are met. Yet under this system, a person dying of a treatable illness has no greater claim to care than a person seeking treatment for a hangnail. One might observe that the distribution is formally fair but substantively unjust. What the background cases possess and the medical lottery lacks is responsiveness to the morally relevant features of particular cases — the severity of need, the gravity of the wrong, the depth of the disadvantage. It appears that the definition, by confining justice to procedural requirements, cannot distinguish distributions that are genuinely just from those that are merely consistent.

background_cases: When the American colonies declared independence from British rule, they asserted not merely the absence of particular impositions but the capacity to govern themselves — to make their own laws, set their own taxes, and determine their own affairs; their conception of freedom included the positive capacity for self-governance, not merely the negative condition of non-interference.

When the labour movement of the nineteenth century demanded the right to organise, it sought not merely the removal of legal prohibitions on assembly but the positive capacity to bargain collectively — to exercise real power in the determination of working conditions; freedom meant not merely being unobstructed but being empowered to act.

When newly independent states in the mid-twentieth century asserted their sovereignty, they claimed not merely freedom from colonial domination but the capacity to chart their own economic and political course — to build institutions, develop resources, and participate as equals in international affairs; their freedom was constituted by the capacity to act, not merely by the absence of external control.
definition: A person or group G is free if and only if (i) G is not subject to coercion or domination by any other person or group, (ii) G is not legally prohibited from pursuing any course of action that does not harm others, and (iii) no institution exercises arbitrary power over G.
conditions: G is not subject to coercion or domination
G is not legally prohibited from harmless courses of action
No institution exercises arbitrary power over G
counterexample: Consider a small, newly independent nation that has freed itself from colonial rule. It is not subject to coercion or domination by any external power. Its citizens face no legal prohibitions on harmless activities. No institution exercises arbitrary power over them. All three conditions are met. Yet the nation lacks the trained personnel, the institutional infrastructure, and the economic resources to govern itself effectively. It cannot build schools, maintain roads, administer courts, or provide medical care. It is free in the sense that no one obstructs it, yet it is unable to exercise the self-governance it has won. One might observe that its freedom is purely formal — the absence of interference without the capacity to act.
abductive_insight: The background cases share a feature absent from the newly independent but incapacitated nation: in each, freedom is constituted not merely by the absence of external interference but by the positive capacity to act — to govern, to bargain, to build institutions. The American colonies sought the capacity for self-governance. The labour movement sought the capacity to bargain collectively. The newly independent states sought the capacity to develop their own resources and institutions. The newly independent nation in the case above satisfies every negative condition — no coercion, no prohibition, no arbitrary power — yet lacks the positive capacity that, in the paradigmatic cases, constitutes the substance of freedom. The definition captures the absence of obstruction but omits the presence of capability.
passage:
The most celebrated assertions of freedom in political history share a feature that the mere absence of interference cannot capture. When the American colonies declared independence, they sought not merely the removal of particular impositions but the positive capacity to govern themselves — to make their own laws, levy their own taxes, and determine the course of their own affairs. When the nineteenth-century labour movement demanded the right to organise, it sought not merely the removal of legal prohibitions on assembly but the real capacity to bargain collectively — to exercise effective power in the determination of wages and working conditions. When newly independent states in the twentieth century asserted their sovereignty, they claimed not merely freedom from colonial domination but the capacity to build institutions, develop resources, and participate as equals in international affairs. In each case, freedom was constituted not by the absence of obstruction alone but by the presence of the capacity to act.

One might attempt to characterise freedom as the absence of coercion, the absence of legal prohibition on harmless activities, and the absence of arbitrary institutional power. This negative definition captures important conditions for freedom. Yet a case which reveals its insufficiency arises when one considers a small, newly independent nation that has succeeded in throwing off colonial rule. It is subject to no coercion. Its citizens face no legal prohibitions. No institution exercises arbitrary power over them. All three conditions are met. Yet the nation lacks the trained personnel, the institutional infrastructure, and the economic resources to govern itself. It cannot build schools, administer justice, or provide basic services. It is free in the sense that nothing obstructs it, yet it is unable to exercise the self-governance for which it fought. What the background cases possess and the incapacitated nation lacks is the positive capacity to act — to govern, to organise, to build. It appears that the definition, by confining freedom to the absence of obstruction, cannot distinguish the freedom of those who possess the capacity to act from the merely nominal freedom of those who are unobstructed but powerless.

background_cases: When a judge sentences a convicted criminal, we accept the judge's authority because she occupies a constitutionally established office, her decisions are constrained by statute and precedent, and the system that conferred her authority derives from the consent of the governed; authority is constituted by institutional role, legal constraint, and democratic derivation.

When a military officer orders a regiment to advance, we accept his authority because he holds a rank within a chain of command established by lawful government, his orders are bounded by the rules of engagement and the laws of war, and his commission derives from a legitimate civilian authority.

When a headmaster sets the rules of a school, we accept his authority because his position was established by a governing board, his rules are bounded by educational regulations, and the governing board itself derives its authority from the community it serves.
definition: An institution or officeholder X possesses legitimate authority over persons Y if and only if (i) X occupies a role established by rules that are themselves accepted as valid by the relevant community, (ii) X's directives are bounded by the scope of the role and by overriding legal or moral norms, and (iii) the system that confers X's role ultimately derives from the consent or acceptance of those subject to it.
conditions: X occupies a role established by rules accepted as valid by the relevant community
X's directives are bounded by the scope of the role and by overriding norms
The system conferring X's role ultimately derives from the consent of the governed
counterexample: Consider a colonial governor appointed by a distant imperial power to administer a territory whose inhabitants had no voice in the establishment of the colonial system and have never consented to its authority. The governor occupies a role established by rules that are accepted as valid by the imperial metropole. His directives are bounded by colonial law and by the administrative scope of his office. Yet the system that confers his role does not derive from the consent of the governed — the colonised population was never consulted. The first two conditions are satisfied from the perspective of the imperial power, but condition (iii) fails with respect to the governed population. Nevertheless, the governor exercises effective authority: he levies taxes, administers courts, and commands a police force.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved authority systems in which the consent of the governed and the institutional role of the officeholder were aligned — the judge's authority derived from a system the citizenry accepted, the officer's commission derived from a legitimate civilian government, the headmaster's position derived from a community governing board. The definition captured this alignment but could not accommodate cases in which the institutional role is valid within one community (the imperial power) while the persons subject to the authority (the colonised) are entirely excluded from the system that confers it.
passage:
Three cases illuminate the structure of legitimate authority. A judge sentences a criminal, and we accept her authority because her office is constitutionally established, her decisions are constrained by law, and the constitutional system derives from the consent of the governed. An officer orders an advance, and we accept his authority because his rank exists within a lawful chain of command derived from civilian government. A headmaster sets school rules, and we accept his authority because his position derives from a governing board accountable to the community. In each case, institutional role, legal constraint, and democratic derivation converge. One might therefore define legitimate authority as the occupation of a role established by accepted rules, bounded by overriding norms, and ultimately derived from the consent of the governed.

Yet this definition is challenged by a case that has been of enormous significance in modern political history. Consider a colonial governor appointed by a distant imperial power to administer a territory on the other side of the world. The governor occupies a role established by rules that are accepted as valid — by the parliament and civil service of the metropole. His directives are bounded by the scope of his office and by the colonial legal code. The first two conditions are satisfied. Yet the system that confers his role does not derive from the consent of the governed population. The inhabitants of the colony were never consulted about the establishment of the colonial system. They had no voice in the appointment of the governor. They did not participate in the creation of the laws by which they are administered. Condition (iii) fails with respect to the persons who are actually subject to the governor's authority. One might observe that the background cases from which the definition was drawn all involved situations in which the persons subject to authority were also members of the community from whose consent the authority derived. The definition captured this coincidence of the governed and the consenting but could not anticipate their divergence in the colonial case, where authority is exercised over one population by virtue of the consent of an entirely different one.

background_cases: In a small New England township, citizens gather in a meeting hall and vote directly on local ordinances; we regard this as democratic because every citizen participates, the votes are equal, and the majority decision binds the community; democracy is constituted by direct, equal participation in collective decision-making.

In a parliamentary system, citizens elect representatives who debate and vote on legislation; we regard this as democratic because the representatives are chosen by the people, are accountable through periodic elections, and act as agents of the electorate's will.

In a referendum, citizens vote directly on a proposed constitutional amendment; we regard this as democratic because the decision is made by the people themselves, each vote counts equally, and the outcome reflects the collective preference of the electorate.
definition: A political decision D is democratically legitimate if and only if (i) D is made, directly or through elected representatives, by the citizens affected by D, (ii) each citizen's input carries equal weight in the decision, and (iii) D reflects the preference of the majority of participants.
conditions: D is made by the citizens affected by D, directly or through representatives
Each citizen's input carries equal weight
D reflects the preference of the majority of participants
counterexample: Consider a city in which a majority of citizens vote to rezone a neighbourhood inhabited exclusively by a particular ethnic minority, converting their residential district into an industrial zone and effectively forcing them from their homes. The decision is made by the citizens through a free vote. Each vote carries equal weight. The outcome reflects the majority preference. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the decision is an act of majoritarian oppression directed at a vulnerable minority, and one might observe that calling it democratically legitimate evacuates the concept of democracy of its moral content.
abductive_insight: The background cases all involved decisions whose effects were distributed roughly evenly across the participating population — local ordinances, general legislation, constitutional amendments affecting all citizens. The definition captured the procedural features of these cases but dropped the implicit assumption that the majority's decision would not be directed against a specific minority as its target. When the majority acts not to govern the community but to dispossess a particular group, the procedural features remain intact while the spirit of democratic self-governance is betrayed.
passage:
Three cases suggest that democratic legitimacy resides in the procedural features of collective decision-making. In a New England township, citizens gather and vote directly on ordinances; in a parliamentary system, elected representatives legislate on behalf of the people; in a referendum, citizens vote on constitutional questions. In each case, the decision is made by the affected citizens, each citizen's input is equal, and the outcome reflects the majority preference. One might therefore define a democratically legitimate decision as one made by the affected citizens, with equal input, reflecting the majority preference.

Yet this definition is embarrassed by a case of the following sort. Consider a city containing a neighbourhood inhabited predominantly by members of a particular ethnic minority. A majority of the city's citizens, drawn from outside the neighbourhood, vote to rezone the district from residential to heavy industrial use. The effect of the rezoning would be to render the neighbourhood uninhabitable, effectively forcing its residents from their homes. The decision is made by the citizens through a free and fair vote. Each vote carries equal weight. The outcome reflects the preference of the majority. All three conditions of the definition are met. Yet the decision is manifestly an act of majoritarian oppression — the use of democratic procedure as an instrument for the dispossession of a vulnerable minority. One might observe that the background cases from which the definition was drawn all involved decisions whose effects were distributed broadly across the participating population. The ordinances, legislation, and constitutional amendments affected everyone more or less equally. The definition captured the procedural structure of these cases but implicitly assumed that the majority's decision would not be a weapon aimed at a specific group. When that assumption is violated, the procedural features remain intact while the moral foundation of democratic legitimacy — the idea that the people govern themselves rather than oppress one another — is betrayed.

background_cases: When a court sentences two persons convicted of the same offence under the same circumstances to the same penalty, we regard the outcome as just because like cases have been treated alike; justice is evidenced by the consistent application of a common standard.

When a tax system requires every citizen to pay the same percentage of income, we regard it as just because the same rule applies to all; the proportionality ensures that the burden is distributed according to a uniform principle.

When an examination is marked by the same criteria for all candidates, we regard the results as just because every candidate has been measured against the same standard; justice is evidenced by the uniformity of the evaluative framework.
definition: An outcome O is just if and only if (i) O results from the application of a rule that applies equally to all persons in the relevant class, (ii) the rule was applied consistently — that is, without arbitrary exceptions or favouritism, and (iii) the persons affected by O were aware of the rule before it was applied.
conditions: O results from the application of a rule applying equally to all persons in the relevant class
The rule was applied consistently without arbitrary exceptions
The persons affected were aware of the rule before it was applied
counterexample: Consider a famine-stricken community in which the sole remaining food is distributed according to a rule that applies equally to all, is consistently enforced, and was announced in advance: each person receives exactly one kilogram of grain per week. Adults and children, the healthy and the gravely ill, pregnant women and the elderly — all receive the same allocation. The rule is applied without exception or favouritism. All three conditions are satisfied. Yet the outcome is not just: the identical allocation fails to meet the vastly different nutritional needs of different members of the community. A kilogram of grain sustains a healthy adult but is insufficient for a nursing mother and excessive for an infant. Formal equality of treatment produces substantive inequality of outcome.
